On its face, the Gaels’ loss to Gonzaga didn’t alter the big picture. Saint Mary's is still the West Coast Conference tournament top seed and will see its name as a high seed on Selection Sunday. But the way the Bulldogs did whatever they wanted against a Gaels’ squad that built its winning formula on its defense was troubling. They’ll undoubtedly see the Zags again, so they’ll have to figure out how to get points when they can’t get stops.

The Gators’ loss to South Carolina was by no means fatal, but it did damage their chances for a top-four spot and double bye in the SEC tournament. It also raised questions about where their points might come from when facing defense-first teams, which they’re sure to encounter in the Big Dance.

search_result.title: Coronavirus: NCAA seniors fell anguish of missing March Madness
search_result.text:
March 15, 2020, 11:03 a.m. ET

As reality sinks in, the pain is also beginning to take full effect for college basketball seniors.

In the aftermath of the NCAA tournament being cancelled due to the coronavirus pandemic, those players are coming to grips with their careers ending abruptly, costing them an opportunity to experience their own One Shining Moment.

"Once the tournament was cancelled, it took me a while to realize my career was over just out of nowhere," said Utah State senior guard Sam Merrill, who hit a game-winning three-pointer to help the Aggies upset San Diego State in the Mountain West Tournament final — claiming the league's automatic bid to the NCAAs. "Everything we worked for last summer and this season was based on getting back to the NCAA tournament, and it's tough to have that opportunity taken away by an unfortunate circumstance out of our control. We felt like we could do something special. Now that's gone."

More than 800 college basketball players would have participated in the bracket of 67 games across 14 sites in the NCAA tournament's three weeks starting Tuesday. With March Madness serving as the pinnacle of players' seasons and careers, Thursday's news brought a sudden and shocking end.

"Suddenly, the game isn't there anymore," said Belmont senior Tyler Scanlon, who never got to play in the NCAA tournament. "It didn't happen the way it was supposed to. We didn't lose. That's what makes all of this so eerie."

Some players will work and wait a year for a chance to play in March Madness. But seniors with no more eligibility are done.

"For all college basketball seniors who were ready to play in the NCAA tournament, this one hits hard," said Yale senior guard Eric Monroe, another to never play in March Madness. Yale was granted an automatic bid to the NCAA tournament when the Ivy League became the first conference to cancel its tournament because of concerns about coronavirus.

"It's just sad. I'm definitely a nostalgic person. My childhood was obsessed with the NCAA tournament, filling out brackets and watching all the games. It was my favorite time of year. I felt so close to living out that dream by playing in a March Madness game. At the end of the day though, basketball is just a game. People are dying. It's easy to get wrapped up in emotion but knowing there's a bigger picture out there has helped me cope."

The NCAA on Friday announced that Division I athletes could regain lost eligibility for spring sports seasons that were canceled due to the pandemic. College sports' governing body is also considering enacting similar waivers for athletes in winter sports — including men's basketball.

But some seniors played full seasons outside of the NCAA tournament. Would the NCAA let them come back for a full or half season?

"My five seniors will never get to experience hearing their name called on Selection Sunday, creating memories that will last forever, that they could tell their kids about," East Tennessee State coach Steve Forbes said. His Buccaneers were a projected No. 10 seed in the NCAAs. "In my opinion, every senior should have the right to come back. I know it's not going to happen. But you know what it's how I feel. They had this experience taken way from them and they've got nothing to do with it."

For some seniors, even an extra year of eligibility couldn't erase the pain from last week's cancellation. Dayton, for example, was a projected No. 1 seed with a 29-2 record, including 18-0 in the Atlantic 10. Trying to replicate a near-perfect season is a tall task, especially when national player of the year front-runner Obi Toppin is a projected NBA draft lottery pick likely to leave campus.

"I wish all of this was just a dream that I could wake up from," Dayton senior Trey Landers said. "Wish I could play one more game with my brothers."

Just getting to the tournament is a main goal for mid-major teams. Belmont's Scanlon transferred to the Bruins as a graduate student after playing at Boston University. He hit the game-winning shot in the Ohio Valley Conference Tournament final win over Murray State. That was supposed to be a ticket to the NCAAs. It instead became the final shot of his career.

"Especially if you play in a one-bid conference, it's like you have two Super Bowls — winning your conference tournament to get to the NCAA tournament and then winning several games in the tournament," Scanlon said. "Some of the power five schools might be more focused on winning a national championship. For us, it's more about the opportunity. I think the thing that everybody loves about March Madness is the Cinderellas and the underdogs — when the No. 12 seeds make a run. That's what America will miss the most and we're sad we can't be the ones to bring it to them."

Bradley senior guard Darrell Brown said he was fortunate enough to play in the NCAA tournament last year when the Braves nearly upset Michigan State as a No. 15 seed. They won the Missouri Valley Conference Tournament again this year — which now serves as the end to his career.

"I'm a senior, so obviously I have a lot of different emotions," Brown said. "In a way, it is heartbreaking because it feels like unfinished business. Playing in the NCAA tournament last year was the best experience of my life. ... We felt like we could upset some teams and go on a run this year. But this is a team sport and I know the legacy I leave some of the younger players is an impression of what it feels like to be a winner. You try to rest knowing you left something behind."

Merrill agreed with that notion for the conclusion of his career at Utah State. "We didn't quite reap the benefits we would have liked," he said. "But I do feel like there are things that can't be taken away. If I had known that the NCAA tournament would be cancelled last summer, I still would have put in all the work. I wouldn't trade that from a life character standpoint. It was all worth it."

search_result.title: Florida-Auburn score: Walter Clayton Jr., Gators to NCAA ...
search_result.text:
Updated April 5, 2025, 9:27 p.m. ET

March Madness continued Saturday with the men’s Final Four games from the Alamodome in San Antonio, Texas.Florida is headed back to the NCAA Tournament national championship game for the first time since 2007 after a Final Four victory over SEC rival Auburn.

The Gators will play on Monday against the winner of Saturday's second Final Four game, which pits No. 1 seed Duke against No. 1 seed Houston.

Florida’s Walter Clayton Jr. entered the game leading the Gators with 18.1 points and 4.2 assists per game. The star was clutch in this game, dropping 34 points to lead the game's scorers.

USA TODAY had full coverage of today's Auburn vs. Florida game. Here are the scores, highlights and how it all went down:

Auburn vs. Florida final score, results

Florida defeat Auburn 79-73.

Florida came out strong with a scoring run that has helped keep the game close against Auburn. The Gators led Johni Broome and the Tigers with two minutes left in regulation after trailing by as many as nine points at halftime. The Tigers outscored the Gators 26-14 in the paint in the first half.

When is the Men’s National Championship game?

The men's NCAA National championship game will take place on Monday, April 7, with an 8:50 p.m. ET tip-off time.

The game will be played at the Alamodome in San Antonio, Texas.

Walter Clayton Jr. stats

Walter Clayton Jr. had 34 points, four rebounds and two assists for Florida.

He is the first player with back-to-back 30-point games in the Elite Eight and national semifinals since Larry Bird in 1979, according to ESPN.

Florida vs. Auburn: Final Four Highlights

Johni Broome stats

Johni Broome has 15 points and seven rebounds with one assist, three steals and two blocks for Auburn.

Johni Broome injury update: Auburn star dealing with elbow, ankle vs Florida in Final Four

Have the Auburn Tigers ever won a national championship?

The Auburn Tigers have never won a national championship. They have reached as far as the Final Four in 2019. The program also claims two Elite Eight appearances (1986, 2019) and six Sweet 16 appearances.

Where is the Final Four? What to know about Alamodome

The Alamodome, opened in May 1993, is owned by the city of San Antonio. Named after the Alamo, it has a capacity of 65,000 and welcomes the 2025 Final Four. It previously served as the home of the San Antonio Spurs and also houses the annual Alamo Bowl.

Charles Barkley cheers for Auburn

Auburn alumnus Charles Barkley was seen cheering for the Tigers during the Final Four game against SEC rival Florida.

Johni Broome injury update

During that game, he managed to overcome an injury scare in the second half that sent him to the locker room for X-rays for some time. He did return to finish the game.

Auburn vs. Florida all-time head-to-head record, history

The Auburn Tigers and Florida Gators have played one another 174 times. Auburn enjoys a 90-84 record in the series. The Gators most recently defeated the Tigers 92-81 on Feb. 8, 2025.

Florida Gators national championship, NCAA tournament history

The Florida Gators have won the national championship twice (2006, 2007). The Gators also went to the finals in 2000, losing to Michigan State, 89-76.

Florida vs. Auburn preview

Florida’s first priority will be keeping Auburn’s star big man Johni Broome under wraps. The Gators have a number of options there, as frontcourt starters Alex Condon and Rueben Chinyelu have been joined by key reserve Micah Handlogten, another seven-footer who had planned to redshirt but rejoined the team in mid-February to provide needed depth. Broome, for his part is still likely to post yet another double-double by night’s end, and Dylan Cardwell can help on the glass. But the Tigers will need points from the arc to keep pace with the Gators. Tahaad Pettiford has had a good tournament thus far, but the Tigers need better shot selection from Miles Kelly can continued success from Denver Jones.

Ah yes, about those Gators’ scorers. As we’ve seen, it will probably be Walter Clayton Jr. taking the big shot when needed, which means he’s likely to draw attention from Auburn’s Chad Baker-Mazara. But Florida also has Elijah Martin, who has Final Four experience from his time at Florida Atlantic, to help alleviate the ball pressure on the perimeter. — Eddie Timanus

Final Four by the numbers: Metrics predict No. 1 seed boon

All hail Ken Pomeroy; the king of the calculator, the seer of the stats, the architect of the analytics and the executive of efficiency.

For more than a decade, a website run by a former meteorologist and atmospheric sciences instructor has been the numbers-based Bible of college basketball. Pomeroy’s formula ranks teams by a variety of different metrics including offensive efficiency, defensive efficiency, tempo and luck. But his top-line number assigned to each team, calculated from data over thousands of possessions across the season, is pretty much the gold standard in terms of predicting on a game-to-game basis which team is more likely to win. — Dan Wolken

search_result.title: Local products, York College stars pile up postseason accolades
search_result.text:
Updated Nov. 14, 2023, 4:07 p.m. ET

A bevy of York College athletes and York County natives have garnered all-conference honors this month following exceptional 2023 fall seasons. This week, two seniors were recognized after starring for their hometown Spartans.

York College volleyball’s Abby Diehl, a Dover graduate, earned a MAC Commonwealth first-team selection Monday. Teammate Gianna Krinock, a former West York state champion who joined the Spartans as a graduate student this year, garnered an honorable mention alongside sophomore Morgan Nolte.

Diehl finished her career as a three-time all-conference honoree — she was a second-team pick in 2021 and an honorable mention last year — and she closed with her best season yet. The fifth-year senior registered 776 assists (7.92 per set) and a league-best 73 service aces while adding 62 kills and 226 digs.

Krinock played her previous two seasons at Elizabethtown after beginning her career at Virginia Wesleyan in 2019, and she had her best statistical season as a Spartan. She set a career high with 301 kills and a .213 hitting percentage while adding 232 digs. Krinock twice earned MACC weekly honors as she reached her top form late in the season.

Nolte, a Middletown product, also claimed two weekly honors during the season and ranked third in the conference with a .343 hitting percentage and fifth with 73 total blocks. With York College losing Diehl and Krinock among other seniors from this year’s 17-12 squad, Nolte figures to be a centerpiece for the program’s future.

More college volleyball: Penn State York captures third place at national championships

Also among the MACC honorees is Widener’s Shannon Toomey, a York Catholic graduate who received an honorable mention from the league. The junior registered 204 kills, a .342 hitting percentage and 47 total blocks for the Pride.

The NCAA Division III volleyball tournament begins this week, with Dallastown graduate Sydney Ohl hoping to help lead 29-0 Juniata to a repeat national championship. In Division II, the tournament bracket will be revealed Sunday following conference tournament action around the country, including in the PSAC.

Field hockey: Before York College took the field in the NCAA D-III Tournament, seven Spartans received MAC All-Commonwealth honors last Wednesday. Goalie Belle Fields was named the league’s Co-Defensive Player of the Year, while teammates Katie Martino, Riley Buschert and Sara Pique all made the first team. Pique was selected for the third time and the other three are now two-time first teamers. Maggie Spadafora (West York) and Brooke Cordisco were picked to the second team, while Libby Wild was an honorable mention.

Elsewhere in the Commonwealth, Messiah freshman Aubrey Clark (Red Land) was named the MACC Rookie of the Year and a first team all-conference selection. Clark had nine goals and three assists for the Falcons, who wrapped an 18-2 season with Sunday’s NCAA quarterfinal loss to Middlebury.

In the D-II PSAC, Kutztown sophomore Grace Harrold (Central York) made the league’s second team. She and fellow Central graduate Breann Craley helped lead the No. 3 Golden Bears to the semifinals of the six-team NCAA Tournament with a 2-1 win over Pace last Saturday. Craley scored in the victory, which set up a semifinal clash with No. 2 Shippensburg this Friday in New Hampshire.

Men’s soccer: York College senior Luke Nall and freshman Dylan Marco both earned MACC second-team nods last Friday. Nall, who was a first-teamer in 2022, had a goal and four assists from the midfield for the 6-7-3 Spartans. Marco started the final seven games of the season and York went 4-3 with him in the lineup. York is slated to return its entire starting lineup in 2024.

Alvernia senior Gabe Wunderlich (Dallastown) was a Commonwealth first-team selection after tallying seven goals — including five game-winners — and three assists for the Golden Wolves. He also made the MACC’s first team in 2022 but moved from forward last year to midfield this season.

Northern York graduate Connor Swope was a PSAC East first-team honoree for Lock Haven, while Millersville’s Jake Altimore (West York) made the second team.

Women’s soccer: Freshman Meg Kalthof led a deep Spartan contingent of honorees, claiming MACC Offensive Player of the Year and Co-Rookie of the Year last Thursday after leading the league with 27 points (11 goals, five assists). Teammates Morgan Joyce, Alyssa Russell and Sara Frensley all joined Kalthof on the first team, while Ashlynn Weger (Susquehannock) was a second-team pick following a five-goal, four-assist campaign.

Two Y-A products were recognized by the PSAC, with Millersville’s Isabel Coy (York Suburban) and Shepherd’s Lydia Gable (Kennard-Dale) both named to the East Division’s second team.

MORE SPARTAN STANDOUTS

After spending her freshman year of college away from the game of basketball, Eastern York graduate Breana Grim is back in a big way for York College. The sophomore guard was named MACC Women’s Player of the Week after scoring 18 and 16 points in her first two collegiate games. Grim went 13-of-20 from the field and 6-of-12 from 3-point range in Coaches vs. Cancer Classic contests against Juniata (a 70-42 win) and No. 20 Mary Washington (a 64-59 loss).

The Spartan men’s team, meanwhile, saw sophomore guard Jayden Rowe earn MACC Player of the Week honors after leading York to the Mike Daley Memorial Tournament title at Nazareth (Rochester, N.Y.) over the weekend. Rowe scored 31 points in the championship game as York knocked off Nazareth, 72-68, and opened the weekend with 22 points and eight rebounds in a 92-68 victory over SUNY Canton.

Sophomore Isabella Klemm was named MACC Women’s Swimmer of the Week for her performance in York’s 122-103 loss at Washington College. Klemm won the 50 free, 100 back and 200 back as an individual while also leading off for the winning 200 medley relay team.

search_result.title: College basketball: Wichita State thriving; Duke may be done
search_result.text:
March 6, 2021 Updated March 7, 2021, 1:41 a.m. ET

When Wichita State parted ways with longtime coach Gregg Marshall — the man that helped the program reach the 2013 Final Four and transition to the American Athletic Conference — the idea of playing in the 2021 NCAA Tournament felt like an afterthought.

But here we are in March, and Wichita State players are cutting down nets, having just won a share of the AAC regular season with a comfortable victory Saturday over South Florida. The win also shifts WSU to a much safer projected No. 10 seed and further from the wrong side of the NCAA Tournament bubble.

Marshall resigned after an investigation into allegations he had physically and verbally abused players became public.

Coach Isaac Brown, who spent six years under Marshall as an assistant, took over as the interim in November and then was offered the full-time gig with a five-year contract.

Less than a week after the Shockers' season ended last March, a walk-on and six scholarship players announced their decisions to transfer. This year, WSU faced several interruptions due to COVID-19. Despite five game postponements since the end of January, WSU hasn't lost since Jan. 21 and has won seven in a row.

Winners

Wichita State. The Shockers rolled over South Florida 80-63 and now are building a case that will make it difficult for the committee to keep them out of the NCAA Tournament. The problem with WSU's profile is a NET score in the 60s. A top-25 non-conference strength of schedule should ultimately work in this group's favor.

Connecticut. The Huskies (14-6, 11-6 Big East) improved upon their No. 10 seed they started the day off with by handling Georgetown 98-82 behind James Bouknight's 21 points. When Bouknight, the team's leading scorer, is on the court this team has lost just two games. The selection committee considers injuries and that should make UConn relatively safe right now for its inclusion in the field.

Rutgers. The Scarlet Knights (14-10, 10-10 Big Ten) defeated Minnesota 77-70 to essentially end the Gophers' chances of an at-large bid. In Rutgers' case, it's coming off a loss to Big Ten bottom-feeder Nebraska, so winning is essential to avoid major sweating on Selection Sunday.

Drake. The Bulldogs (25-3, 15-3 MVC) survived Missouri State for a 71-69 win in the MVC Tournament semifinals. Had Drake lost, it likely would have been sweating big time on Selection Sunday. Instead, now it faces Loyola-Chicago, ranked No. 22 in the Ferris Mowers Coaches Poll, for the Valley tournament final. Should Drake not claim the league's automatic bid, its credentials are still good enough to squeak in as a No. 11 or No. 12 seed.

Saint Bonaventure. The Bonnies (15-4, 12-4 Atlantic 10) defeated another A-10 bubble team in Saint Louis, 71-53, which likely pushes the Billikens out of the conversation. In Saint Bonaventure's case, this win helped keep it relatively safe in the No. 11 seed range. Two Quadrant 1 victories aren't flashy enough, but a top-30 non-conference strength of schedule and top-30 NET score will be difference-makers on Selection Sunday.

Virginia Commonwealth. Currently projected as a No. 11 seed, the Rams helped solidify their NCAA Tournament case by advancing to the A-10 tournament championship game with a 64-52 win over Davidson. Though VCU can guarantee its entry into the Big Dance by beating St. Bonaventure in the title game, the Rams are aided by a top-35 NET ranking. – Jace Evans

Utah State. The Aggies are one of the "First Four Out" in the latest projections, but they avoided a stumble by closing out their regular season slate with a 57-51 victory over Fresno State. Utah State sports a top-50 NET ranking and enters the Mountain West Tournament, which it's won the past two seasons, on a four-game winning streak.

OPINION: Look no further than the Big Ten for your national titlist

WINNERS AND LOSERS: Oklahoma State surging; Villanova falters

ACC CHAMPIONS: Reigning NCAA Tournament titlist Virginia atop ACC

BIG TEN SHOWDOWN: No. 4 Illinois may have wrapped up No. 1 seed

Losers

Duke. The Blue Devils (11-11, 9-9 ACC) badly needed a Quadrant 1 win and failed to get it in a 91-73 road loss to archrival North Carolina. That's Duke's third loss in a row after a four-game winning streak resuscitated the team's NCAA Tournament hopes. Now it's going to take a deep run in the ACC tournament for coach Mike Krzyzewski's group to have a chance. A NET score in the high 50s and just two Quad 1 wins isn't enough to entice the committee. Duke hasn't missed the tournament since 1995.

Louisville. The Cardinals (13-6, 8-5 ACC) have a deceptive profile and with Saturday's 68-58 loss to Virginia, now they're in hot water. Starting the day as a No. 10 seed, a loss to the ACC's top team wasn't surprising, but it was a missed opportunity to inch toward the safety zone heading into the ACC tournament. Now coach Chris Mack's group will need to get at least one more win to secure its inclusion. There's only one Quad 1 win on the profile and a NET score of 49.

Indiana. Consider Indiana's postseason hopes dashed unless coach Archie Miller can inspire this group to a deep run in the Big Ten tournament. The Hoosiers (12-14, 7-12) fell 67-58 to Purdue for their fifth consecutive loss — the wrong recipe for a bubble team in March. A NET score of 59 and losing record just won't cut it, even playing in the Big Ten.

Georgia. The Bulldogs (14-11, 7-11) lost to the best team in the SEC, Alabama, 89-79. That might've been expected, but it was also Georgia's major shot at vaulting to the right side of the bubble by acquiring a marquee victory. Instead, Georgia's on life-support and its March Madness hopes will have to be resuscitated with a deep run in the SEC tournament.

Follow college basketball reporter Scott Gleeson on Twitter @ScottMGleeson

search_result.title: March Madness 2025 Selection Sunday updates, brackets, predictions
search_result.text:
March 16, 2025 Updated March 17, 2025, 1:11 p.m. ET

It's over. It's done.

As Selection Sunday draws to an end, the book has closed on debates on seeding. Now, let the matchup debates begin.

Sunday brought about a few surprises. West Virginia and Indiana were left out of the tournament while North Carolina and Texas got in as First Four teams. Michigan and Louisville were also seemingly somewhat surprised at their No. 5 and No. 8 seedings, respectively.

Auburn drew the No. 1 overall seed, leading the SEC's record-breaking 14 teams. Duke sat at No. 2 overall atop the East bracket. Houston and Florida round out the No. 1 seeds atop the Midwest and West brackets, respectively.

Who will cut down the nets in San Antonio? We're a ways and a few upsets away from finding out. But it's time to brace, for there will be madness. USA TODAY Sports provided the latest men’s bracket reveal updates, conference tournament highlights, news, analysis and more throughout the day. Recap here.

Iowa State's Keshon Gilbert will not play in NCAA Tournament

It appears Keshon Gilbert's time with Cyclones is done.

Gilbert, who averaged 13 points in 30 minutes per game with Iowa State this season, is going to miss the men's NCAA Tournament with a strained groin that has kept him out of four of the past seven games for T.J. Otzelberger's squad.

Read more here from the Des Moines Register's Eugene Rapay.

CBS analysts pick Florida, Auburn and Duke to win national championship

While the ESPN crew unanimously picked Florida to win the national championship, the CBS crew was a bit more diverse in their national champion pick. Clark Kellogg picked Florida over Clemson, while Seth Davis picked Duke over the Gators.

Finally, someone who did not have Florida in the championship game, Jay Wright, picked Auburn over Houston. Wright did not have the Gators in the Final Four.

ESPN picks Florida to win national championship

Florida earned a No. 1 seed by winning the SEC tournament championship game on Sunday over Tennessee. The Gators and coach Todd Golden have gained a lot of respect over the season. So much so, Rece Davis, Jay Bilas, Seth Greenberg and Jay Williams all picked Florida to win national title this year.

Bilas picked Florida over Houston in his national championship, while the three others picked the Gators to beat Duke.

Bruce Pearl says Tigers are "very pleased" with No. 1 overall seed

Despite closing the season with three losses in its final four games, Auburn earned the No. 1 overall seed in the tournament. Still, the Tigers had a strong regular season and earned the top seed in a conference that sent a record 14 teams to the NCAA Tournament.

Auburn coach Bruce Pearl told the media that his players were "very pleased" with the No. 1 overall seed.

"We sort of feel like that makes us the regular-season national champion," Pearl said.

SEC sets tournament record with 14 teams in March Madness

Much was made about how impressive the SEC was this year, and the conference backed it up on Selection Sunday.

With Texas' inclusion in First Four play, the SEC sent 14 teams to March Madness, breaking the previous record of 11 set by the Big East in 2011.

Jay Wright picks his Final Four

Former Villanova coach Jay Wright knows a little something about tournament runs.

The two-time NCAA Tournament champion and four-time Final Four coach picked his Final Four in Sunday's show, picking three No. 1 seeds (Houston, Duke, and Auburn) and Rick Pitino's St. John's to make it to San Antonio.

NCAA Tournament field seeded 1-68

While the No. 1 overall seed tends to get the most recognition, every team 1-68 gets its ranking. Here's a look at the full seeding.

NCAA selection committee's vice chair discusses North Carolina's inclusion

Bubba Cunningham joined CBS' bracket reveal show to discuss North Carolina's inclusion. Hover, as North Carolina's athletic director, Cunningham was not involved in conversations about the Tar Heels' case.

As such, vice chair Keith Gill fielded the question of how North Carolina was included, dropping the bombshell that North Carolina would have been left out if UAB had defeated Memphis to steal a bid.

No. 1 seed in the West goes to Florida

The Gators might be the best team entering the tournament but still gets kicked out West despite winning the SEC tournament. St. John's also heads across the country after a Big East regular-season and conference title and misses out on playing near home in Newark, N.J. An amazing first-round matchup of coaches sees Kansas and Bill Self face Arkansas and John Calipari. Four SEC teams are in the region, giving the league a record 14 for the tournament. The previous mark was 11. Texas Tech and Maryland are two teams that could prove dangerous to the top seeds. Two-time defending champion Connecticut will look to keep its tournament magic going, but getting out of Raleigh will be difficult.

In Raleigh, N.C.

No. 1 Florida vs. No. 16 Norfolk State

No. 8 Connecticut vs. No. 9 Oklahoma

In Seattle

No. 5 Memphis vs. No. 12 Colorado State

No. 4 Maryland vs. No. 13 Grand Canyon

In Wichita, Kan.

No. 6 Missouri vs. No. 11 Drake

No. 3 Texas Tech vs. No. 14 UNC Wilmington

In Providence, R.I.

No. 7 Kansas vs. No. 10 Arkansas

No. 2 St. John's vs. No. 15 Nebraska-Omaha

Houston leads the Midwest after Big 12 title

The Cougars get a break by staying in region with the Big 12 regular-season and conference tournament titles in hand, edging Florida. No. 2 Tennessee is seeking its first Final Four and could see No. 3 Kentucky in the Sweet 16. The Wildcats beat the Volunteers twice in SEC play. Xavier is another surprise entry to the field with only one Quad 1 win. Seems the committee valued success in conference play more than wins, though Texas getting a selection for that play-in game rewards their big wins but poor schedule. The Longhorns had 15 losses, equaling the most for any team getting an at-large berth.

In Wichita, Kan.

No. 1 Houston vs. No. 16 Southern Illinois-Edwardsville

No. 8 Gonzaga vs. No. 9 Georgia

In Providence, R.I.

No. 5 Clemson vs. No. 12 McNeese State

No. 4 Purdue vs. No. 13 High Point

In Milwaukee, Wis.

No. 6 Illinois vs. No. 11 Texas/Xavier

No. 3 Kentucky vs. No. 14 Troy

In Lexington, Ky.

No. 7 UCLA vs. No. 10 Utah State

No. 2 Tennessee vs. No. 15 Wofford

Duke takes the No. 1 seed in the East

This a familiar path for the Blue Devils, having come out of the East Region before. The health of Cooper Flagg is key, though it seems he should be good for next week and probably won't be needed until the regionals in two weeks. Some interesting matchups loom ahead. No. 2 Alabama is a high-scoring team that runs hot and cold. Wisconsin is very solid as a No. 3, and Brigham Young is among the best teams with the most momentum entering the tournament and are only a No. 6 seed. We could see former Pac-12 rivals Oregon and Arizona match up in the second round.

In Raleigh, N.C.

No. 1 Duke vs. No. 16 American/Mount St. Mary's

No. 8 Mississippi State vs. No. 9 Baylor

In Seattle

No. 5 Oregon vs. No. 12 Liberty

No. 4 Arizona vs. No. 13 Akron

In Denver

No. 6 Brigham Young vs. No. 11 Virginia Commonwealth

No. 3 Wisconsin vs. No. 14 Montana

In Cleveland

No. 7 Saint Mary's vs. No. 10 Vanderbilt

No. 2 Alabama vs. No. 15 Robert Morris

Auburn is the No. 1 overall seed in the South

The Tigers enter after losing three of four games but still land as the top seed in the tournament and stay home in the South. Michigan State and Iowa State will be two defensive-minded teams that could upset the Tigers. Texas A&M - one of the teams that beat Auburn in the home stretch - looms as a possible Sweet 16 opponent. The surprise here is North Carolina making the play-in game as one of the final at-large teams despite a 1-12 record in Quad 1 games. Michigan wins the Big Ten tournament and has to turn around and play Thursday against a dangerous UC San Diego team in Denver.

In Lexington, Kentucky

No. 1 Auburn vs. No. 16 Alabama State/Saint Francis (Pa.)

No. 8 Louisville vs. No. 9 Creighton

In Denver

No. 5 Michigan vs. No. 12 UC San Diego

No. 4 Texas A&M vs. No. 13 Yale

In Milwaukee

No. 6 Mississippi vs. No. 11 San Diego State/North Carolina

No. 3 Iowa State vs. No. 14 Lipscomb

In Cleveland

No. 7 Marquette vs. No. 10 New Mexico

No. 2 Michigan State vs. No. 15 Bryant

How to stream Selection Sunday

Stream: March Madness Live | Paramount+ | Fubo (free trial)

Streaming options for the NCAA Tournament selection show include March Madness Live, which can be accessed through the NCAA's website, Paramount+ — which requires a subscription to access — and Fubo, the last of which carries CBS and offers a free trial to potential subscribers.

Michigan claims Big Ten championship

Another automatic bid has been claimed, with Michigan beating Wisconsin in the men's Big Ten tournament final 59-53. The Wolverines are conference tournament champions after Michigan State won the regular-season title.

Big Ten championship: Michigan, Wisconsin tied at 53

Michigan and Wisconsin are tied at 53 with 1:34 left in the Big Ten Conference tournament championship game.

USA TODAY Sports compiled pay information from each school in the Power Five conferences and from each school outside those conferences whose team has appeared in at least three of the past five NCAA tournaments.

While Kentucky has had moments this season, including a late run that has lifted the Wildcats toward the top of the SEC, the results and payoff given the school's mammoth investment into Calipari and the program makes him yet again one of the most overpaid coaches in the country. Read Paul Myerberg’s full story here.

SEC Championship game underway

The SEC tournament championship game is underway, starting a string of major conference championship games on Selection Sunday. Florida has jumped to a 10-6 lead at the first media timeout. The Gators are on a 7-0 run, looking to wrap up a No. 1 seed with another win.

Five most underpaid men's college basketball coaches: Lamont Paris, Matt Painter make list

Hired as the head coach of the Chattanooga Mocs in 2017, Lamont Paris won only a combined 22 games in his first two seasons before things took off. In his final season, 2021-22, the Mocs went 27-8, won the Southern Conference regular-season and tournament championships, and nearly topped Illinois as a No. 13 seed in the men's NCAA tournament, losing 54-53.

It took only two seasons for Paris, 49, to do the same at South Carolina. After going 11-21 in his debut year, Paris has the Gamecocks at 25-6 and a No. 5 seed in USA TODAY Sports' latest bracketology update.

How much would you be willing to spend on a coach capable of such a rapid turnaround? While 19 Division I coaches are earning at least $4 million in salary, Paris will make $2.3 million in total compensation in 2023-24.

USA TODAY Sports compiled pay information from each school in the Power Five conferences and from each school outside those conferences whose team has appeared in at least three of the past five NCAA tournaments. Here are the coaches providing the best return on investment, beginning with Paris at South Carolina.

SEC championship game: Florida leads at halftime

Denzel Aberdeen hit a 3-pointer from 28 feet out at the buzzer to end the first half, giving Florida a 39-30 halftime lead. The Gators have led for much of the first half, including a lead of as much as 12 points.

Will Richard has a game-high 11 points to lead Florida. Jordan Gainey leads the Vols with 10 points. Tennessee shot 38.5% from the field in the first half, while Florida shot 45.2%. The Gators outscored the Vols 16-4 in the paint.

SEC championship: Tennessee hanging around

Despite Florida leading for much of the game and by as many as 13 points, Jordan Gainey has 20 points and helped cut the deficit to just five points. However, Chaz Lanier picks up his fourth foul and Will Richard knocks down free throws to push the Florida lead to 62-55 with 8:47 remaining.

SEC championship: Florida wins first championship since 2014

Florida beats Tennessee 86-77 in the SEC championship game to collect the program’s first SEC tournament championship since 2014. Todd Golden becomes the only other coach outside of Billy Donovan to win a conference championship with the Gators.

Walter Clayton Jr. finished with 22 points in the win for Florida, which clinched a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament with the win. Will Richard added 17 points and Alex Condon had 13 points and nine rebounds.

VCU wins Atlantic 10 championship; Bracket update

VCU beat George Mason 68-63 in Sunday's Atlantic 10 championship game. The Rams were already a team that was going to make the tournament, so their win is good news for bubble teams.

According to Jeff Borzello, Joe Lunardi said three teams ― Indiana, Xavier, Boise State ― are competing for two spots in the tournament. Lunardi said North Carolina and Texas are eliminated.

Big Ten championship: Wisconsin holds slim halftime advantage

Following a sluggish offensive first half for both teams, Wisconsin leads Michigan 23-21 in the Big Ten Conference tournament championship game. The Badgers shot 8 of 37 (21.6%) from the field, while the Wolverines shot 9 of 32 (28.1%).

John Blackwell leads all scorers with 11 points for Wisconsin, while Vladsil Goldin leads Michigan with seven points.

When is men’s Selection Sunday 2025?

The 68-team bracket for the 2025 NCAA men’s basketball tournament will be unveiled on Sunday, March 16.

What channel is Selection Sunday on?

The 2025 NCAA Tournament selection show will air on CBS, its traditional broadcast home. Streaming options include Paramount+, CBS's streaming site, and Fubo, which offers a free trial to potential subscribers.

What time does Selection Sunday start?

Time: 6 p.m. ET

The 2025 NCAA Tournament selection show is scheduled to begin at 6 p.m. ET on CBS, though it could be delayed if the Big Ten Tournament championship game runs long.

March Madness 2025 schedule

The 2025 NCAA men’s basketball tournament will transpire over the next three weeks, which will end with the Final Four and the national championship game in San Antonio.

Here’s a rundown of the schedule for the 2025 NCAA Tournament:

First Four : March 18-19

: March 18-19 First round : March 20-21

: March 20-21 Second round: March 22-23

March 22-23 Sweet 16 : March 27-28

: March 27-28 Elite Eight : March 29-30

: March 29-30 Final Four : April 5 at the Alamodome in San Antonio

: April 5 at the Alamodome in San Antonio National championship game: April 7 at the Alamodome in San Antonio

Bracket Challenge 2025: How to enter USA TODAY Sports Bracket Challenge with a chance to win $1 million

The USA TODAY Sports Bracket Challenge is back, with a $1 million grand prize for a perfect bracket. The Challenge is free to enter for those age 21 and over. Terms apply, void where prohibited by law. See official rules and get in the game.

How to watch March Madness 2025

The 2025 NCAA men’s basketball tournament will air on CBS and the Turner family of networks, including TBS, TNT and truTV. Streaming options include NCAA March Madness Live, Paramount+, Sling TV – which carries the Turner family of networks – and Fubo, which carries CBS and offers a free trial.

Here's a breakdown of what games are on what channels, per Turner Sports and the NCAA:

CBS will broadcast 24 games throughout the tournament including the national championship, Final Four semifinals, Elite Eight, Sweet 16 and first and second rounds.

TBS will televise 18 games, including the Elite Eight, Sweet 16 and first and second round games.

TruTV will air or simulcast a total of 21 games, including the First Four games, Elite Eight, Sweet 16 and first and second round games.

TNT will televise 12 games, including First and Second round matchups.

2025 men's NCAA Tournament automatic bids: Who has secured spots in March Madness?

Each of the 31 conference tournament champions earns an automatic berth to the NCAA Tournament, with the other 37 spots awarded to at-large participants by the tournament’s selection committee.

For the overwhelming majority of mid- and low-major leagues, the automatic berth from winning the conference tournament is the league’s only bid. Here’s a look at the champions of each of the 31 conference tournaments, all of which are on their way to the NCAA Tournament:

America East: Bryant

Bryant ACC: Duke

Duke ASUN: Lipscomb

Lipscomb Atlantic 10: VCU

VCU Big 12: Houston

Houston Big East: St. John's

St. John's Big Sky: Montana

Montana Big South: High Point

High Point Big Ten: Michigan

Michigan Big West: UC San Diego

UC San Diego CAA: UNC Wilmington

UNC Wilmington Conference USA: Liberty

Liberty Horizon League: Robert Morris

Robert Morris Ivy League: Yale

Yale MAAC: Mount St. Mary's

Mount St. Mary's MAC: Akron

Akron MEAC: Norfolk State

Norfolk State Missouri Valley: Drake

Drake Mountain West: Colorado State

Colorado State Northeast: St. Francis (Pa.)

St. Francis (Pa.) Ohio Valley: SIU Edwardsville

SIU Edwardsville Patriot League: American

American SEC: Florida

Florida SoCon: Wofford

Wofford Southland: McNeese

McNeese SWAC: Alabama State

Alabama State Summit League: Omaha

Omaha Sun Belt: Troy

Troy WAC: Grand Canyon

Grand Canyon West Coast: Gonzaga

What day does March Madness start?

The NCAA Tournament First Four on the men’s side begins on Tuesday, March 18 and continues on Wednesday, March 19.

The four men’s games, which are held in Dayton, Ohio, feature the final four at-large selections to the field, as well as the four lowest-rated No. 16 seeds. The winner of each matchup advances to the first round. First-round games will take place on March 20 and March 21.

When is the first March Madness game?

The first men’s game of the 2025 NCAA Tournament will be on March 18 with the start of the First Four.

March Madness 2025 game locations

First- and second-round games will be held at eight different cities spanning across each of the four time zones in the continental United States. Sweet 16 and Elite Eight games will each be held at different regional sites before the final four teams converge on San Antonio in early April.

Here’s a look at where 2025 NCAA men’s basketball tournament games will take place:

First Four : Dayton, Ohio | UD Arena | March 18-19

: Dayton, Ohio | UD Arena | March 18-19 First/second round : Lexington, Kentucky | Rupp Arena | March 20-22

: Lexington, Kentucky | Rupp Arena | March 20-22 First/second round : Providence, R.I. | Amica Mutual Pavilion | March 20-22

: Providence, R.I. | Amica Mutual Pavilion | March 20-22 First/second round : Wichita, Kansas | Intrust Bank Arena | March 20-22

: Wichita, Kansas | Intrust Bank Arena | March 20-22 First/second round : Denver | Ball Arena | March 20-22

: Denver | Ball Arena | March 20-22 First/second round : Seattle | Climate Pledge Arena | March 21-23

: Seattle | Climate Pledge Arena | March 21-23 First/second round : Milwaukee | Fiserv Forum | March 21-23

: Milwaukee | Fiserv Forum | March 21-23 First/second round : Raleigh, N.C. | Lenovo Center | March 21-23

: Raleigh, N.C. | Lenovo Center | March 21-23 Sweet 16/Elite Eight : Newark, N.J. | Prudential Center | March 27-29

: Newark, N.J. | Prudential Center | March 27-29 Sweet 16/Elite Eight : San Francisco | Chase Center | March 27-29

: San Francisco | Chase Center | March 27-29 Sweet 16/Elite Eight : Atlanta | State Farm Arena | March 28-30

: Atlanta | State Farm Arena | March 28-30 Sweet 16/Elite Eight: Indianapolis | Lucas Oil Stadium | March 28-30

Indianapolis | Lucas Oil Stadium | March 28-30 Final Four : San Antonio | Alamodome | April 5

: San Antonio | Alamodome | April 5 National championship: San Antonio | Alamodome | April 7

Bracket challenge 2025: How to enter and win USA TODAY Sports' March Madness Survivor Pool

Check out the USA TODAY Survivor Pool game hub, where we're committed to bringing a little extra excitement to your March Madness experience. Showcase your knowledge of the best teams across college basketball by picking winners. Survive the longest to win cash and other prizes. Best of all, it's completely free to play. Register today for your chance to participate in the best survivor pool experience around!

How does Selection Sunday work?

The 68-team NCAA Tournament field is put together by the tournament selection committee on both the men’s and women’s side. The committee determines not only who gets in the field – with 31 automatic berths for conference tournament champions and 37 at-large bids – but what each squad is seeded and where their games are set to take place.

The final brackets are revealed on nationally televised shows, with the men’s selection show airing on CBS and the women’s selection show taking place on ESPN.

Here’s a closer look at how Selection Sunday works.

How many teams make March Madness?

There are 68 teams that make both the men’s and women’s NCAA Tournament. The final four at-large teams, as well as the four lowest-rated No. 16 seeds, will take part in the First Four.

Who plays in March Madness?

The 68-team tournament on both the men’s and women’s side is made up of 31 conference champions, who earn an automatic bid to March Madness by winning their league tournament, as well as 37 at-large teams that are picked by the NCAA Tournament selection committee.

How are March Madness bids determined?

Thirty-one of the 68 spots in the NCAA Tournament field are automatic bids earned by teams that win their conference tournament.

Things get a little thornier when it comes to the final 37 at-large spots, which are determined by the NCAA Tournament selection committee. A team’s worthiness to be one of those 37 selections is determined by a variety of metrics, including a team’s record, its marquee wins and the strength of its schedule.

There’s no set of statistics more important than the NCAA’s NET rankings, which college sports’ governing body devised and deployed ahead of the 2018-19 season, replacing the Ratings Percentage Index (RPI). Most notably, a team’s games are divided into four quadrants, which are determined by the quality of an opponent and where the game was played (whether at home, on the road or at a neutral site). Here’s a closer look at how the quadrants work, with the listed numbers reflecting an opponent’s ranking:

Quadrant 1 : Home 1-30, Neutral 1-50, Away 1-75

: Home 1-30, Neutral 1-50, Away 1-75 Quadrant 2 : Home 31-75, Neutral 51-100, Away 76-135

: Home 31-75, Neutral 51-100, Away 76-135 Quadrant 3 : Home 76-160, Neutral 101-200, Away 135-240

: Home 76-160, Neutral 101-200, Away 135-240 Quadrant 4: Home 161-353, Neutral 201-353, Away 241-353

How are March Madness seeds determined?

The NCAA Tournament selection committee seeds teams one through 68, with squads getting placed into groups of four to determine their seed. Teams ranked from one through four, for example, are No. 1 seeds, teams five through eight are No. 2 seeds and so on.

A team’s ranking is based on a variety of different factors — its overall record, its number of Quad One wins, its strength of schedule and how many, if any, Quad Three or Quad Four losses it has. Since the NET rankings were first employed during the 2018-19 season, the committee has prioritized Quad One wins over perhaps any other measurement when assessing seeds.

How is it determined where teams play in March Madness?

Once the 68 teams are selected and seeded for the NCAA Tournament, the selection committee has to sort out which of the predetermined locations in the country each squad will play.

The event’s highest seeds, particularly the Nos. 1 and 2 seeds, will typically get games close to the school’s campus for the first and second rounds. The No. 1 seeds then get placed in a region for the Sweet 16 and Elite Eight that makes the most geographic sense, though that doesn’t always apply to the third- and fourth-ranked No. 1 seeds. Last year, for example, North Carolina was the last of the No. 1 seeds and was placed in the West Region, which held Sweet 16 and Elite Eight games in Los Angeles.

If Duke, as expected, ends up with a No. 1 seed, the Blue Devils would likely get first- and second-round games in nearby Raleigh, North Carolina, a short drive from the school’s Durham campus.

There are competitive considerations that come into play, as well. When the selection committee ranks the tournament’s 68 teams, it attempts to bracket the team in a snake order, meaning that the No. 5 overall team — which is the best of the No. 2 seeds — ends up in the same region as the No. 4 overall team (the lowest-rated No. 1 seed). There are restrictions, though, such as:

search_result.title: Purdue basketball: Pregame vs. Texas Tech in East Region semis
search_result.text:
July 31, 2017 Updated March 23, 2018, 4:49 p.m. ET

BOSTON — Purdue basketball faces Texas Tech in an East Region semifinal at TD Garden tonight in pursuit of its first Elite Eight appearance since 2000. The TBS broadcast will begin at approximately 10 p.m. after the conclusion of the Villanova-West Virginia semifinal.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue, the 2 seed in the East Region, advanced to Boston by defeating Cal State Fullerton in Detroit. The Boilermakers are in the Sweet 16 for the second straight season.

Texas Tech, the 3 seed, advanced by defeating Stephen F. Austin and Florida in Dallas.

DAKOTA MATHIAS: Senior sets the tone for Purdue basketball with his calm intensity

JACQUIL TAYLOR: Reserve forward helped make his Sweet 16 homecoming happen

STARTING FIVE

1-Purdue needs to win this game defensively. Keenan Evans is a considerable talent and, even playing through his injury, limiting him won’t be easy. The Boilermakers need to win the matchups at the other four spots.

2-Evans’ biggest potential impact on this game would be getting Matt Haarms in early foul trouble. It would force Purdue to either go small for long minutes or give them to Jacquil Taylor. Not saying he couldn’t rise to the occasion, but that’s an unproven concept right now.

3-Carsen Edwards’ efficiency has waned a bit over the last three games. While that may be the law of shooting averages working themselves out to some degree, he’ll need to pick his spots well tonight. How will Texas Tech’s longer defenders affect his shooting?

4-Nojel Eastern didn’t play as much against Butler, but he seems like a potentially important piece against a team with Tech’s guard length. Can he sub in and add that burst of offensive assertiveness when Purdue needs it?

5-I’ve talked all season about mature wins for this team. Marquette, Arizona, Louisville, at Michigan, Butler X 2. They’ll need one more than ever tonight. On this stage, with these stakes, keeping a level head against Tech’s pressure will be critical. It’s a challenge that goes on the backs of Dakota Mathias, P.J. Thompson and Vincent Edwards.

March 18: Time, TV announced for Texas Tech in Sweet 16

No. 2 seed Purdue’s East Region semifinal against 3 seed Texas Tech will tip off at approximately 10 p.m. Friday at Boston’s TD Garden. TBS will carry the broadcast.

That game follow’s the region’s first Sweet 16 contest between 1 seed Villanova and 5 seed West Virginia.

Pregame vs. Butler in NCAA Tournament

DETROIT — Purdue basketball plays for a return trip to the Sweet 16 today in an NCAA Tournament East Region round of 32 game against Butler at Little Caesar’s Fieldhouse. The CBS broadcast begins at 12:10 p.m.

For updates and analysis, follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue, the 2 seed, advanced with Friday’s 74-48 victory over 15 Cal State Fullerton. Butler, the 10 seed, upset 7 seed Arkansas 79-62 on Friday.

The Boilermakers defeated Butler 82-67 on Dec. 13 in the Crossroads Classic at Bankers Life Fieldhouse. Purdue led by 26 points with under 14 minutes to play. Carsen Edwards’ 18 points led five Boilermakers in double figures. Purdue held Butler to 37.6 percent field goal shooting but allowed 18 offensive rebounds and committed 18 turnovers.

Isaac Haas played through foul trouble in that game by scored 16 points in 15 minutes. However, Purdue ruled Haas out of the rest of the tournament after he suffered a fractured right elbow in the win over Cal State Fullerton.

Haas as expected emerged from Purdue’s locker room for warm-ups wearing a significant brace on his right arm. He grimaced and looked extremely hesitant when attempting any right-handed shot more than a few feet out.

He stood at the free throw line and lined up to shoot, but never actually attempted a shot.

From what he showed in warm-ups, regardless of Haas’ health, his mobility at this point displayed that he would not be an asset to Purdue today and would very likely be a liability.

Matt Haarms is starting at center for Purdue.

Purdue sought permission from the NCAA officials for Haas to wear that brace for the elbow, but coach Matt Painter said that was to allow Haas to participate in warm-ups. Painter reiterated Saturday he does not expect the senior center to play again this season with surgery necessary.

Butler’s announced that Paul Jorgensen, who was a game-time decision after suffering an ankle injury in Friday’s win over Arkansas, will play today.

ON BUTLER: Bulldogs must hit from deep if another Sweet 16 run is in the cards

STARTING FIVE

1-I expect Isaac Haas to have a presence with Purdue on Sunday. He’ll be in uniform, from what we’ve been told, and he’s expected to try to participate in warm-ups. But I’d be surprised if he plays in a meaningful away again this season. Painter said Saturday that Haas could not grab a rebound two-handed, which says a lot about his motor limitations at this point.

2-Purdue needs to carry forward Friday’s defensive performance. The Boilermakers handled ball screens much better and bottled up some potentially dynamic perimeter options for Fullerton. The other wrinkle now is Matt Haarms can provide the same extended rim-protecting presence he did in the first win over Butler.

3-However, he must do so without fouling. Are there scenarios where going small at the 5 can be advantageous for Purdue, especially offensively? Yes. Are the scenarios where Haarms sitting most of the first half with foul trouble turns into a disaster for the Boilermakers. Yes again. There’s only so much confidence Purdue can have in someone like Jacquil Taylor right now simply because he hasn’t had an opportunity to prove himself.

4-Another big matchup to watch is Vincent Edwards (I presume) against Kelan Martin. That was the assignment for the Crossroads Classic meeting. Keeping up with such a talented wing will further test the soundness of Edwards’ ankle. Things looked good Friday, and the day off helped, but it’s still something to watch.

5-Purdue also must carry forward the second-half ball movement from Friday. The first half against Fullerton too closely resembled a Wisconsin-like scenario. Quick jump shots and a lack of flow. Then at times in the second half the Boilermakers looked as locked in as at any point this season. The competition level raises Sunday, and they can’t afford another extended period of stagnation.

DETROIT – Purdue basketball opens NCAA Tournament play as the 2 seed against 15 seed Cal State Fullerton in the Detroit quadrant of the East Region. The truTV broadcast begins at 12:40 p.m.

For updates and analysis during the game follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

The Boilermakers will be playing their first game in 12 days since the Big Ten Tourrnament championship game loss to Michigan. Fullerton has had a six-day layoff since its Big West Tournament championship victory over UC Irvine.

ISAAC HAAS: Purdue center maximizing his effort over maximum minutes

STARTING FIVE

1-Whatever rustiness Purdue might experience due to the layoff should be offset by the benefits of the break, especially Vince Edwards’ health. More than whether shots fall, watch how the Boilermakers move the ball. That’s the top indicator of offensive success.

2-Purdue must defend these assertive Fullerton guards without getting into foul trouble on the perimeter. If it doesn’t, it may also lead to foul trouble on the interior. Forcing the Titans to take jump shots will be key.

3-At some point, Fullerton will throw a small lineup out there, sliding 4 man Jackson Rowe (6-7, 210) down to the post and using a smaller 4, possibly guard Khalil Ahmad. He played the 4 for the Titans last year in the Big West tournament.

4-Ball security and shot selection will be crucial as Purdue doesn’t want to play into the Titans’ preference for running the floor and scoring in transition. These sorts of matchups also present rare opportunities for the Boilermakers to own the boards.

5-Purdue should have more fans in the arena, but in the NCAA Tournament, that doesn’t necessarily mean a home court advantage. Unaffiliated fans will trend towards the underdog if the game is close late.

March 4: Pregame vs. Michigan in Big Ten Tournament championship

NEW YORK – No. 8 Purdue basketball plays for its second Big Ten Tournament championship today against No. 13 Michigan. The CBS broadcast begins at 4:30 p.m.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue defeated Penn State 78-70 on Saturday in the semifinals. Carsen Edwards scored 27 points after scoring 26 in a quarterfinal victory over Rutgers.

Michigan advanced by defeating Michigan State in Saturday’s semifinals.

Purdue defeated Michigan twice this season, winning 70-69 in Ann Arbor and 92-88 at Mackey Arena. The Wolverines beat the Boilermakers in the quarterfinals of last year’s tournament.

PURDUE 78, PENN STATE 70: Boilermakers advance to Big Ten tournament championship game

PURDUE 82, RUTGERS 75: Both Edwardses score 26 in quarterfinal win

STARTING FIVE

1-Keep an eye on Vincent Edwards’ left ankle. He played 72 minutes the past two days and he didn’t have the same pep Saturday as he did Friday. He scored 30 against Michigan at Mackey and Purdue needs his rebounding help but he also must be smart for the long-term, even with the week off next week.

2-Purdue wanted to win the Big Ten Tournament to help its bid for a No. 1 seed. Even if it wins today, I’m not sure that win, on top of wins over Penn State and Rutgers, will move the needle much. The Boilermakers may need some upset help around the country next week to climb into the top four on the seed line.

3-Possibly as early as today, Dakota Mathias will be Purdue’s all-time 3-point leader all by himself. He shares the distinction with E’Twaun Moore after making two yesterday. That’s quite a distinction considering how many shots Mathias has passed up over the past four years.

4-Muhammad-Ali Abdur-Rahkman hit six 3-pointers and scored 26 points at Mackey Arena. He’s hit 8 of 11 in the past two games of this tournament. He’ll be an important defensive matchup today, presumably for Carsen Edwards.

5-Mo Wagner already fouled out of one game in this tournament – against Iowa in the second round. He had four or more in four of the last five games of the regular season. He had four in both games against Purdue. Can the Boilermakers use Isaac Haas to take Michigan’s best frontcourt weapon off the floor?

March 3: Pregame vs. Penn State in Big Ten Tournament semifinals

NEW YORK – No. 8 Purdue basketball plays in the Big Ten Tournament semifinals for the third time in four years against Penn State. The CBS broadcast begins at approximately 4:30 p.m.

For updates and analysis during the game, follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue defeated Rutgers 82-75 in Friday’s quarterfinals. Carsen Edwards and Vincent Edwards scored 26 points apiece.

Penn State upset Ohio State 69-68 in the quarterfinals. The Nittany Lions ended a three-game losing streak with Thursday’s win over Northwestern.

PURDUE 82, RUTGERS 75: Purdue holds off Rutgers to advance in Big Ten Tournament

STARTING FIVE

1-Vincent Edwards looked great last night, and that he could play 37 minutes in any way was a good sign for Purdue. The next big test comes today, when he has to play a second straight day on that still-healing left ankle. It’s not just a matter of keeping a baseline of production through the discomfort or lack of footing – Edwards has to avoid making the issue worse. On the other hand, with no games next week, he’ll have extra time to heal up from whatever wear and tear occurs this weekend.

2-With Mike Watkins out with injury for Penn State, Purdue can’t get outrebounded the way it did against Rutgers. The Nittany Lions held a 35-28 rebounding edge in last month’s game in West Lafayette. Those deficits aren’t necessarily a problem if the Boilermakers aren’t giving away points off turnovers – which they also did against Rutgers.

3-Can Purdue contain Shep Garner better this time around? He hit five 3-pointers and scored 17 points in the first meeting. He averaged seven points over his first five career games against Purdue.

4-Did stamina make a difference in Purdue’s favor late in the win over Rutgers? Will it potentially make a bigger difference today against a Penn State team playing its third game in as many days?

5-Corey Sanders shrugged off P.J. Thompson’s defensive influence last night, but he needed 20 field goal attempts to score 23 points and turned the ball over four times. Shutdown performance? No. But Thompson’s veteran savvy made an impact even before he took that pivotal charge late.

NEW YORK CITY – No. 8 Purdue basketball begins Big Ten Tournament play tonight against Rutgers. The BTN broadcast will begin at approximately 9 p.m., or 25 minutes following conclusion of the Ohio State-Penn State game.

For updates and analysis during the game, follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue won three straight games to end the regular season. Rutgers has already won twice in this tournament, upsetting Minnesota and Indiana. The Scarlet Knights finished last in the Big Ten during the regular season and lost twice to the Boilermakers.

NOJEL EASTERN: Purdue freshman making strides for the distant and immediate future

PURDUE 78, RUTGERS 76: Purdue survives serious upset scare at last-place Rutgers

STARTING FIVE

1-Not the quarterfinal you were expecting? Especially after Indiana led Rutgers 24-8? As Matt Haarms so eloquently said earlier this week, “We don’t want to get March Madnessed.” Rutgers has been madnessing left and right here at MSG this week.

2-Which carries more weight, Purdue’s blowout win at home or the narrow victory in Piscataway? All things being equal I’d say the second game, because it was more indicative of the Scarlet Knights’ full potential. Rutgers still doesn’t shoot all that well, but it will create space for its playmakers and attack with pick and rolls, etc.

3-Rutgers nearly upset Purdue on Feb. 3 in part because it made shots at a higher rate than almost any other game this season. (Remember, also, this was during Vincent Edwards’ squeamish phase.) What can the Boilermakers do defensively to interrupt the quick, athletic Scarlet Knights?

4-Rutgers took Isaac Haas away in the second meeting, allowing him only five field goal attempts and four free throws in 27 minutes. It’s the only game since Jan. 20 that Haas hasn’t scored in double figures. Purdue needs to either get him more involved or capitalize more from the perimeter. It made 7 of 21 from 3 in Piscataway.

5-Will be intriguing to see how Vincent Edwards looks today. He was clearly not 100 percent against Minnesota in the regular-season finale but had some extra emotion to play with. Presumably a few extra days of rest and treatment should help.

Feb. 25: Pregame vs. Minnesota

No. 9 Purdue will try to end the regular season on a three-game winning streak today against Minnesota at Mackey Arena. The FS1 broadcast begins at 4 p.m.

For updates and analysis during the game follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue will recognize outgoing seniors Vincent Edwards, Isaac Haas, Dakota Mathias and P.J. Thompson after the game.

Edwards is expected to play after missing the last two games with a sprained left ankle. He returned to practice in a limited capacity on Friday and participated more on Saturday. He will likely not be a 100 percent, however, and his minutes could again be limited.

SENIOR NIGHT: Purdue seniors leaving behind legacy of accountability

After today, Purdue does not play again until the Big Ten Tournament quarterfinals on Friday. The Boilermakers will be the No. 3 seed.

Michigan State locked up the outright Big Ten championship with a 68-63 victory over Wisconsin. Purdue can tie Ohio State for second place with a victory.

STARTING FIVE

1-Emotions should be high before, during and after this game. I expect the most sentimental senior night in my five years of covering the program. The challenge may be keeping those emotions in check and playing basketball.

2-The most important thing for Purdue today basketball-wise is to build on the positives of Thursday’s win at Illinois. The Boilermakers moved the ball well throughout the game in spite of Illinois’ intense ball pressure.

3-Edwards had a big day against the Gophers at The Barn in part because they helped with their 4 man on Haas. Will they change that approach, or if Edwards’ minutes are limited, will Minnesota go back to that strategy?

4-Purdue should be motivated to finish on a stronger defensive performance today after surrendering 86 points at Illinois. (Of those points, however, 12 came directly off of Boilermaker turnovers.)

5-Carsen Edwards is coming off the biggest Purdue scoring day in 24 years. One can assume he’ll see even more attention than he might have otherwise. So far this season, that’s been the biggest sign of his growth – his ability to thrive within the offensive flow.

Feb. 22: Pregame at Illinois

Purdue basketball plays its final road game of the Big Ten Conference season tonight at Illinois. The FS1 broadcast begins at 7 p.m.

For updates and analysis during the game, follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue coach Matt Painter said senior forward Vincent Edwards will not play tonight at Illinois.

This will be the second straight game Edwards has missed due to a sprained left ankle suffered last Saturday in practice.

Junior forward Grady Eifert started in Edwards’ place in last Sunday’s 76-73 victory over Penn State and presumably will start again tonight. The matchups across the board against an undersized Illinois team might benefit smaller Purdue lineups with an emphasis on ballhandling against the Illini pressure defense.

Edwards said Wednesday he hoped to return for Sunday’s senior day game against Minnesota, however, he was prepared to sit out if needed to be healthy for the postseason.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Illinois

VINCENT EDWARDS: He and Matt Painter taking the long view on ankle injury

BOILER BENCH: Purdue needs reserves to build on strong effort against Penn State

Without Edwards, Purdue snapped a three-game losing streak with Sunday’s 76-73 victory over Penn State.

Illinois is playing its third game in five days, having beaten Nebraska at home last Sunday and lost at Michigan State on Tuesday.

STARTING FIVE

1-Not surprised at all that Vincent Edwards won’t play. I do think he’ll do everything possible to be on the floor Sunday for the truly once-in-a-lifetime senior day scenario. But nothing Purdue does this week means anything if it doesn’t have a healthy Vincent Edwards next week and beyond.

2-This game has real value for Purdue win or lose. Illinois will bring a lot of pressure and try to force the Boilermakers out of their comfort zone. That’s a scenario the entire roster can benefit from on the eve of the NCAA Tournament, where one must quickly adapt to shifts in strategy and opponents.

3-I’m filling out my All-Big Ten ballot this week and I can’t make up my own mind as to which Purdue player goes highest. Carsen Edwards is making a surge. There’s almost no wrong answer between three guys when you consider Vincent Edwards versatility and Isaac Haas’ impact on opposing strategies.

4-Your “Can’t Let Him Get His Head Up” player of the night is Illinois freshman Trent Frazier. He’s shown more than promise, scoring 32 two weeks ago against Wisconsin. Shots haven’t been falling as frequently the last couple of games. Purdue needs to set a defensive tone there early.

5-This is the game where Purdue’s string of not winning the rebounding battle should end. With both the size and experience advantage and Illinois’ offensive limitations, the Boilermakers won’t have much excuse.

Feb. 18: Pregame vs. Penn State

No. 7 Purdue basketball attempts to break a three-game losing streak against Penn State tonight at Mackey Arena. The BTN broadcast begins at 8 p.m.

For updates and analysis during the game follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue announced before the game that senior forward Vincent Edwards will miss the game with an ankle sprain.

EDWARDS OUT: Forward will miss Penn State game with ankle injury

Purdue is coming off a 57-53 loss at Wisconsin. The Boilermakers are trying to avoid their first losing streak longer than three games since 2013-14.

Penn State defeated Ohio State 79-56 on Thursday. The Nittany Lions have won four straight in pursuit of their first NCAA Tournament berth since 2011.

LOSING STREAK: Purdue seniors take initiative in search for missing swagger

STARTING FIVE

1-If Purdue thought it was playing through adversity before, Vincent Edwards' injury brings some perspective to that narrative. The Boilermakers very simply need someone else to step up and rebound tonight. Mike Watkins collected 11 points, 13 rebounds and three blocks against Purdue in State College last season. I don’t know if the Boilermakers can beat Penn State on the boards, but they can’t afford to get whooped there, especially against a team known for creating turnovers.

2-Ohio State’s loss to Michigan this afternoon may be too little, too late for Purdue’s Big Ten title hopes. The last hope may have trickled away when Northwestern couldn’t hold Saturday’s lead over Michigan State, which now has first place all to itself with two games to play. But honestly, Purdue can’t even consider the standings right now. It simply needs to be begin playing better.

3-Furthering that though, Dakota Mathias said yesterday Purdue hadn’t been enjoying the game as much recently and had “put a lot of pressure” on itself. With things like the Big Ten conference race perhaps unreachable and a No. 1 NCAA Tournament seed also not something they are currently “protecting,” perhaps the Boilermakers play looser tonight. That doesn’t mean out of control or sloppy. Too often they forced the action on offense at Wisconsin, playing tight in scenarios that hadn’t previously vexed them.

4-Purdue held Tony Carr to six points on 2 of 11 shooting while beating the Nittany Lions at Mackey Arena last season. He responded with 21 points and seven assists when Penn State pushed the Boilermakers to overtime at the Bryce Jordan Center. However, he needed 17 shot attempts and didn’t attempt a 3-pointer. Containing Carr will be a priority.

5-The shooting slump can’t last forever – it just can’t. But, it can also return at any time. Purdue both needs the mental boost of seeing some shots fall and the confidence boost of winning even if they don’t.

Jan. 15: Pregame at Wisconsin

No. 7 Purdue tries to break a two-game losing skid tonight at Wisconsin. ESPN’s broadcast begins at 7 p.m.

For updates and analysis during the game follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue is coming off of losses to Ohio State and Michigan State last week. The Boilermakers dropped into a second-place tie in the Big Ten Conference with the Spartans, one game behind the Buckeyes.

Purdue defeated Wisconsin 78-50 at Mackey Arena on Jan. 9. Vincent and Carsen Edwards combined for 41 points and the Boilermakers scored 19 points off of turnovers. They also help Badgers forward Ethan Happ to 15 points on 16 field goal attempts.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Wisconsin

STOCK WATCH: Some positive turnover trends

1-In terms of RPI, none of Purdue’s last four opponents will be much help to an NCAA Tournament resume. However, a proven return to the Boilermakers’ earlier form likely would help the eye test in the minds of committee members. That doesn’t necessarily mean more 30-point blowouts. Purdue keeps putting itself in position to finish with authority but lately has struggled to do so.

2-Will Wisconsin defend Haas straight-up, as has been the trendy approach of late? Or will the Badgers bring help to try and limit foul exposure on Happ?

3-If they go with option B above, can Purdue respond by knocking down some perimeter shots? The Boilermakers, especially Vincent Edwards and P.J. Thompson, are due.

4-Purdue piled up a season-high 15 steals in its first meeting with the Badgers. Just as importantly it limited everyone besides Happ to eight of fewer points. A repeat defensive performance almost assures a victory regardless of Wisconsin’s own defensive plan.

5-Keep an eye on bench production, because increasingly that seems like one of the areas Purdue must galvanize a bit to have a chance for a truly special postseason. Minutes will somewhat dictate the accumulation of points and other statistics. But to protect those leads and extend them, the Boilermakers need a bit more punch from the reserves.

Jan. 11: Pregame at Michigan State

No. 3 Purdue basketball plays its first top-five matchup since 1987 today at No. 4 Michigan State. The ESPN broadcast begins at 4 p.m.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue is coming off a 64-63 loss to No. 16 Ohio State on Wednesday. That ended the Boilermakers’ 19-game winning streak and 12-0 start in Big Ten Conference play. Purdue and OSU are tied for first, one game ahead of Michigan State.

The Spartans have won seven straight after Tuesday’s 96-93 victory at Iowa.

Purdue traveled to East Lansing a day ahead of schedule to avoid inclement weather and was able to practice in Breslin Center on Friday.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Michigan State

GO GREEN VS. BOILER UP: The greatest games in Michigan State-Purdue basketball history

IN MEMORIAM: Purdue legend Gene Keady to pay respects to Jud Heathcote

STARTING FIVE

1-Pretty simple now. The only way Purdue wakes up Sunday morning in control of its own Big Ten destiny is if it wins this game. A loss, assuming Ohio State also beats Iowa at home, means the Boilermakers need help to earn a share of a repeat conference title. (Worth noting OSU’s final four games, including road trips to Penn State, Michigan and Iowa, is tougher than Purdue’s or Michigan State’s. The Spartans also play three of their last four on the road.)

2-At the beginning of the week, Purdue would have taken a win against OSU over a win against MSU because the objective reward – likely the Big Ten title – outweighed the subjective one – a No. 1 NCAA Tournament seed. Now, the postseason implications come to the forefront. As of this morning, Purdue remains a No. 1 seed in the aggregate of 101 predictions on bracketmatrix.com. All but 10 of those still have the Boilermakers as a top four seed. Win or lose, Purdue will have a better sense of where it stands when the NCAA holds its in-season bracket reveal on Sunday.

3-Dakota Mathias (and others) didn’t do a bad job on Keita Bates-Diop on Wednesday. They made the likely Big Ten Player of the Year hit tough shots, and he did. Now Mathias gets a different challenge in Miles Bridges – another All Big Ten-caliber talent.

4-As far as key matchups, Vincent Edwards and Isaac Haas vs. Jaren Jackson and Nick Ward looms large (no pun intended, or perhaps even achieved). Jackson, who played at Indianapolis Park Tudor and had Purdue in his recruiting finalists, has at times struggled with foul trouble. Attacking him there might be key for the Boilermakers, since he’s so key as the Spartans’ defensive backbone.

5-I’ve already written a lot about Purdue’s defensive rebounding vis a vis this matchup. Michigan State’s frontcourt depth sometimes distracts from its guard play. Cassius Winston shoots 52.5 percent from 3-point range. Tum Tum Nairn is a veteran backup presence at point guard. It’s a big assignment for P.J. Thompson, but perhaps a bigger one for Nojel Eastern off the bench.

Jan. 7: Pregame vs. Ohio State

No. 3 Purdue basketball plays to protect first place in the Big Ten Standings against Ohio State tonight at Mackey Arena. The BTN broadcast begins at 8:30 p.m.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue has won 19 straight following a 78-76 victory over Rutgers on Sunday. The Boilermakers are 12-0 in Big Ten play. With a win over Ohio State, Purdue can take a two-game lead with five games to play.

Ohio State has won back-to-back games since suffering its only Big Ten loss, to Penn State on Jan. 25. If the Buckeyes win, they pull into a first-place tie in the Big Ten standings.

BIG WEEK: Purdue needs veteran poise more than ever in week ahead

STOCK WATCH: You missed your chance to buy low on Vincent Edwards

STARTING FIVE

1-Usual caveats apply about taking nothing for granted. But if Purdue wins this game not only does it help that NCAA Tournament No. 1 seed bid, it also probably assures itself no worse than a share of first place in the Big Ten. (Always dangerous to assume but the post-weekend schedule is favorable.) On the other side, Ohio State is paying for a chance to move into first place and put the pressure on Purdue to win at Michigan State on Saturday.

2-Expect Ohio State to throw some small lineups at Purdue. That means the Boilermakers capital M Must capitalize by getting the ball inside to Isaac Haas.

3-Kam Williams’ suspension deprives Ohio State of a 46 percent 3-point shooter. The Buckeyes countered against Illinois by putting a bigger body on the floor. Purdue keeps winning despite being outrebounded in six straight, but it almost cost them at Rutgers.

4-Purdue could obviously use a bounce-back in the 3-point department. Most to the point, Vince Edwards, who is 0-for-his-last-10 from beyond the arc, is due. Will Ohio State back off of him until he proves he has those shooting legs back?

5-Dakota Mathias gets the first assignment on Keita Bates-Diop. But he won’t be the only Boilermaker to match up with the Big Ten Player of the Year frontrunner. Matt Painter listed everyone from Edwards to Grady Eifert to Nojel Eastern as also needing to be ready to handle Bates-Diop.

Feb. 3: Pregame at Rutgers

No. 3 Purdue attempts to extend its 18-game winning streak and remain undefeated in true road games today at Rutgers. BTN’s broadcast begins at 4 p.m.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue is coming off of a 75-67 victory over Maryland on Wednesday. Rutgers has lost four straight games and nine of its last 11.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Rutgers

PURDUE 75, MARYLAND 67: Boilers outlast Maryland to keep winning streak alive

STARTING FIVE
