1-Vincent Edwards is expected to play today despite sitting out yesterday's practice in New Jersey. As sick as Edwards was against Maryland, he still played a team-high 35 minutes with 11 rebound and five assists. He played the entire second half. I’m sure Matt Painter would prefer to rest him in the second half today.

2-Games in the Rutgers Athletic Center usually lack in atmosphere. That may change for today’s announced sellout. Will the Scarlet Knights feed off of that energy?

3-I liked what I saw from Geo Baker in the first meeting at Mackey Arena back on Jan. 3. He’s coming off a 19-point performance at Illinois. Someone to keep an eye on in the Big Ten in the next few seasons.

4-Rutgers ranks 17th nationally in Ken Pomeroy’s adjusted defensive efficiency. Purdue did commit 16 turnovers in the first game. But that defensive effort will need to be spectacular today considering there’s no evidence the Scarlet Knights can win with offense.

5-You know who’s due? Ryan Cline has scored five points over his last three games. He made 4 of 6 from 3-point range against Rutgers earlier this season.

Jan. 31: Pregame vs. Maryland

No. 3 Purdue basketball goes for a season sweep of Maryland tonight at Mackey Arena. The BTN broadcast begins at 8:30 p.m.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue defeated Maryland 80-75 in College Park on Dec. 1 in the Big Ten Conference opener. Dakota Mathias scored 20 points with nine assists. Isaac Haas contributed 21 points and four blocks.

The Boilermakers extended their nation and now program-best winning streak to 17 games with a 74-67 victory at Indiana on Sunday.

Maryland has lost five of its last six, including a 74-68 home loss to No. 4 Michigan State on Sunday. Already missing Justin Jackson and Ivan Bender due to season-ending injuries, Maryland will also be without 7-1 center Michael Cekovsky tonight due to a heel injury suffered Tusday in practice:

Cekovsky has started 18 of 22 games and came in averagingg 7.2 points and 3.5 rebounds.

STARTING FIVE

1-Maryland clearly isn’t clicking right now, but don’t completely dismiss the Terrapins because of their recent skid. It included road games against Michigan State, Ohio State and Michigan. Purdue really had to work to put Maryland away earlier this season. A complete blowout tonight would surprise me.

2-Let’s also not forget what many Purdue opponents from here on in are playing for. Maryland’s NCAA Tournament hopes are fledgling at best. A win at Purdue would be a huge boost.

3-Anthony Cowan and Kevin Huerter remain defensive priorities for Purdue. They combined to make 8 of 14 from 3-point range in the first meeting and went to the free throw line 14 times.

4-Maryland grabbed 16 offensive rebounds in the first meeting. That’s the surest path to an upset bid. The absence of Justin Jackson and Ivan Bender due to injury, however, might limit the Terrapins’ advantage on the boards.

5-Fun streak to watch: 23 games into this season Ryan Cline has not attempted a free throw. (He made 9 of 11 as a freshman, spread over five games.) Other trends: Over his last four games he’s made 7 of 11 from 3-point range with eight assists against no turnovers while playing solid defensive minutes off the bench.

Jan. 28: Pregame at Indiana

No. 3 Purdue travels to Bloomington for its only regular-season meeting with Indiana. FOX will broadcast the 3:30 p.m. tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

The Boilermakers have won five of the last six against Indiana, including three of the last four at Assembly Hall. Purdue swept a home-and-home series last season.

Purdue has won 16 straight games, which is the longest streak in the nation and ties three previous teams for the longest in program history. With a victory today, Purdue can also win 12 straight Big Ten Conference games for the first time, a streak that extends back to last season.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Indiana

ROAD TRIP: Progress of Purdue senior class reflected in road success at Indiana

Indiana will be without redshirt senior forward Collin Hartman, who is not in uniform for today's game.

STARTING FIVE

1-On paper this is a semi-nightmare matchup for IU. It almost certainly has to double the post, and it doesn’t defend the 3 well even in five-on-five situations. The Hoosiers probably have to hope Purdue doesn’t make shots. The Boilermakers made 2 of 18 from 3 in Assembly Hall four years ago and only shot 28.6 percent there last season. (They also won both of those games.)

2-Similarly, defending Juwan Morgan isn’t just a heads-up proposition for Isaac Haas and Matt Haarms. Look for Purdue’s guards to be involved with trying to contain IU’s biggest threat.

3-Purdue gave Michigan due credit for scoring 88 points in Mackey Arena on Thursday. The Wolverines made some crazy tough shots and are in general a tough cover for a lot of teams, not just Purdue. However, the Boilermakers want to be better in ball screen defense and clean up some other areas.

4-With an advantage in multiple facets, Purdue will need to avoid giving up transition opportunities. The Hoosiers do a decent job of creating turnovers. Just as importantly, Purdue needs to keep composure on offense and continue the solid movement and shot selection upon which it has thrived.

5-Weird trend: Purdue has been outrebounded in three straight games (not that there were many for either team to grab in the Michigan game). Hasn’t cost them anything yet but probably something it wants to shore up sooner than later.

Jan. 25: Pregame vs. Michigan

Purdue basketball goes for its first home-and-home season sweep of Michigan since 2008 tonight at Mackey Arena. ESPN will broadcast the 7 p.m. tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

No. 3 Purdue has won 15 straight games, including an 87-63 victory at Iowa last Saturday.

Michigan has won three of its last four, including a 62-47 home victory over Rutgers last Sunday.

Purdue won the first meeting in Ann Arbor on Jan. 9, 70-69. Isaac Haas’ free throw with four seconds left broke the tie.

Boilermakers sophomore guard Carsen Edwards is the reigning Big Ten Conference Player of the Week. He averaged 21.5 points in wins over Wisconsin and Iowa while setting single-game career highs for steals (five) and assists (eight).

ISAAC HAAS: Center making an impact beyond the box score

VINCENT EDWARDS: Forward accelerating his pace as a senior

STARTING FIVE

1-Will this be another nail-biter? No one’s played within arm’s reach of Purdue since the Wolverines took them into the final seconds 16 days ago. The game in Ann Arbor was headed for a similar spread until Michigan stormed back in the second half.

2-The Wagner-Haas matchup wasn’t the key one in the first meeting. Does Purdue have a better answer for Zavier Simpson, not to mention Isaiah Livers and Jordan Poole off the bench. Can the Wolverines shoot as well in Mackey as they did at home?

3-Speaking of Mackey, tonight’s game sets up as perhaps the best atmosphere so far this season. It should be packed and crazy loud. That could affect the Wolverines most on defense, where communication becomes an issue.

4-Carsen Edwards is playing the best basketball of his life on both ends of the floor. He made some huge shots down the stretch of Purdue’s win at Michigan. Do the Wolverines find a way to disrupt him?

5-As of today, beating Michigan at home would “only” be a Tier 2 victory for Purdue under the new NCAA Tournament guidelines. But the Boilermakers’ objective is clear. Multiple players have stated the desire to claim a No. 1 seed, and for the first time in several years that’s a goal within reach. Sweeping the season series from any eventual NCAA Tournament participant would boost the Boilermakers’ case.

Jan. 20: Pregame at Iowa

No. 3 Purdue seeks its 15th consecutive victory today at Iowa. ESPN will broadcast the noon tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue has not won in Iowa City since 2011 – a span of four games. Carver-Hawkeye Arena is one of two remaining Big Ten Conference arenas in which the Boilermaker seniors have never won. (Illinois’ State Farm Center is the other. Purdue won at Michigan’s Crisler Arena for the first time earlier this month.)

Purdue’s winning streak is the longest active streak in the country. Iowa has lost four of its last five after Wednesday’s 80-64 loss at Rutgers.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Iowa

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Seeks better finishes to first halves as streak rolls on

STARTING FIVE

1-Hard to take anything for granted on the road but this obviously seems like a good matchup for Purdue. If the streak of 12 straight games holding an opponent under 70 points continues, hard to see how Iowa springs the upset.

2-Purdue has to lock out second-chance points. The Hawkeyes aren’t very good at much right now, but offensive rebounding is one of the few exceptions. Freshman forward Luke Garza has 12 offensive boards over the past two games.

3-Iowa guard Jordan Bohannon has hit five 3-pointers in each of the past three games (shooting 48.4 percent behind the arc in the process). Presumably that’s P.J. Thompson and/or Carsen Edwards’ assignment. They’ve been quite disruptive against opposing guards over the past couple of games.

4-Does Vincent Edwards remain hot? He’s made 10 of his last 14 from 3-point range (over three games) and scored 20 or more in four of the last six. It’s the sort of surge he typically runs off in March. Is that uptick beginning earlier this season?

5-One more thing on Vincent Edwards: He has a 17-to-4 assist-turnover ratio over the past four games. We’ve obviously talked a lot about how he’s tried to fill Caleb Swanigan’s rebounding role. Lately he’s been doubling down as the purveyor of those kick-outs from the lane.

Nov. 16: Pregame vs. Wisconsin

Purdue basketball returns home after a two-game road swing to play Wisconsin at Mackey Arena. ESPN will broadcast the 7 p.m. tipoff as part of Super Tuesday.

For updates and analysis contact Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue (17-2, 6-0) rose to No. 3 in both the Associated Press and USA Today Coaches Polls after wins at Michigan and Minnesota last week. The Boilermakers received a No. 1 vote in the AP poll for the first time since March 1994.

Wisconsin (9-9, 2-3) is coming off of back-to-back road losses at Rutgers and Nebraska.

The Boilermakers remain tied atop the Big Ten Conference standings with Ohio State. The Buckeyes’ next game comes Wednesday at Northwestern. Every other Big Ten team has lost at least twice.

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Long winning streak keeps Boilermakers focused

STARTING FIVE

1-Purdue dispatched unceremoniously with a wounded opponent on Sunday at Minnesota. Wisconsin has taken a similar (though perhaps not as dramatic) hit personnel-wise. Throughout its 13-game winning streak, the Boilermakers have made a habit of taking care of these matchups emphatically.

2-So what are the Badgers’ best routes to an upset? They’ve done a decent job preventing offensive rebounds and generally limit their turnovers. Wisconsin can’t afford to give away points in either realm and expect to beat a Purdue team surging with confidence.

3-Vincent Edwards admitted the chance to match up against Minnesota’s Jordan Morgan brought out one of those “magic time” performances (to use Matt Painter’s phrase). Will facing Ethan Happ similarly summon a big night from Isaac Haas and/or Matt Haarms?

4-The Boilermakers benefit heavily if this devolves into a free throw battle. That’s another reason to jam the ball inside to the centers.

5-This will be Purdue’s third straight opportunity to showcase itself on an ESPN platform. Those things don’t necessarily have currency in-season. However, the program’s rising ranking and profile do make an impact with recruits and potential recruits.

Jan. 13: Pregame at Minnesota

Purdue takes a 12-game winning streak into today’s game at Minnesota’s Williams Arena. ESPN2 will broadcast the noon tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue is coming off a 70-69 victory at Michigan on Tuesday. The Boilermakers and Ohio State remain the only unbeaten teams in Big Ten Conference play at 5-0.

Minnesota has lost its last two games, including Wednesday’s 83-60 loss at Northwestern.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Minnesota

STARTING FIVE

1-This is a hard game to handicap right now because of the Gophers’ personnel issues. Any team with both Nate Mason and Jordan Murphy remains a talented basketball team. However, Reggie Lynch’s absence inhibits Minnesota’s ability to neutralize the Isaac Haas-Matt Haarms combo. With Amir Coffey out, it’s that much tougher to keep up offensively with a team that can score at Purdue’s level.

2-Mason has enjoyed big performances in his last two matchups with Purdue. This seems like a big moment for both P.J. Thompson and Nojel Eastern coming in behind him. I’d be surprised if he went off for 30 again, and as discussed above, some of the targets of his 25 assists in the last two games won’t be on the floor.

3-Purdue went to the free throw line a season-low seven times against Michigan. Attrition via fouls would obviously work in the Boilermakers’ favor against an already depleted opponent.

4-Vincent Edwards has posted some nice games in The Barn. 24 points, eight rebounds, four assists as a sophomore. 12 points, six rebounds, three assists as a freshman.

5-A trend of note: Per Ken Pomeroy’s site, Purdue has had an effective field goal percentage (weighted for 3-pointers) of at least 61 percent in six of its last seven games. Minnesota has been at 50 percent or lower in four straight and seven of its last eight.

Jan. 9: Pregame at Michigan

Purdue basketball plays its first road game in more than a month tonight at Michigan. ESPN will broadcast the 9 p.m. tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue moved up to No. 7 in the USA Today Coaches Poll and No. 5 in the Associated Press poll on Monday. The AP ranking is the Boilermakers’ highest since they were No. 3 in February of 2010.

Purdue has won 11 straight games, which is tied for the third-longest streak in the country. The Boilermakers and Ohio State lead the Big Ten Conference with 4-0 records.

Michigan has won seven straight and is 3-1 in the Big Ten. It has won four straight at home against Purdue dating back to 2012.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Michigan

STARTING FIVE

1-Obviously stakes change as a season progresses, but this is Purdue’s biggest game since that seventh-place game against Arizona in the Battle 4 Atlantis. In the current Big Ten structure, teams only play a handful of road games against the upper half of the league. Throw in the NCAA tournament implications – this game seems assured to stand up as a Tier 1 contest – and Purdue can gain a lot in two hours tonight.

2-There are no curses at play as to why Purdue hasn’t won at Ann Arbor since 2012. I’ve seen the last three games there in person, and the Boilermakers simply didn’t play well in varying ways. Michigan is always good enough to capitalize on those slip-ups, and that’s true tonight as well. Purdue’s defensive and rebounding strides will be tested.

3-I’m intrigued by the potential Mo Wagner-Matt Haarms matchup. Purdue can switch defensively 1-5 with Haarms in the game, and that flexibility most helps with the matchup against Wagner. But let’s face it – Purdue would prefer to just get Wagner in foul trouble early as it did in the Big Ten Tournament last season.

4-Someone to keep an eye on: Michigan senior guard Muhammad-Abdul Abdur-Rahkman has averaged 14.8 points and shot 56.5 percent from the field over his last five games against Purdue. He scored 25 points in a loss in West Lafayette two seasons ago. He’s reliable with the ball and can shoot a little and is the kind of veteran who can creep up on teams when they otherwise limit the Wolverines’ first options.

5-Will Purdue’s experienced rotation snap into the mindset necessary to thrive in a hostile environment? A solid start – not the gangbusters performance of the Nebraska win, but a good first step – may be critical.

Jan. 6: Pregame vs. Nebraska

Purdue takes a 10-game winning streak into today’s home game against Nebraska. BTN will broadcast the 2:15 p.m. tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

The No. 12 Boilermakers are 3-0 in Big Ten Conference play following Wednesday’s 82-51 victory over Rutgers.

Nebraska is 2-1 in league play after Tuesday’s win at Northwestern and has won four straight.

STOCK WATCH: Rebounding turns in a positive direction

STARTING FIVE

1-I know Nebraska doesn’t raise the blood of Purdue fans the way some other conference foes does. But this is a big game because of what’s ahead next week. Purdue wants to be 4-0 in the league before back-to-back road trips to Michigan and Minnesota.

2-Nebraska has some athleticism and we’ve seen how the Boilermakers have responded (or haven’t) in similar situations. The home environment should help. But perimeter defense and containing players such as Glynn Watson will be important.

3-Along those same lines, Matt Haarms has three or more blocks in each of the past four games. The Cornhuskers’ slashers should give him the opportunity for more today.

4-Purdue won the last two rebounding battles by significant margins. Can that trend continue? Nebraska has been outrebounded for the season.

5-Another important trend: Carsen Edwards has three or more assists in four straight games, and more assists than turnovers in three of those games. Another element of his developing efficiency.

Jan. 3: Pregame vs. Rutgers

No. 13 Purdue basketball returns to Big Ten Conference play tonight against Rutgers. BTN will broadcast the 7 p.m. tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

The Boilermakers have won nine straight. That includes victories over Maryland and Northwestern on the first weekend of Big Ten play in early December.

Rutgers is 10-5 but lost its last two non-conference games to Stony Brook and Hartford.

TRENDING UP: Purdue hopes to continue defensive improvement as Big Ten play resumes

STARTING FIVE

1-Purdue fully expects to find itself in a grinder, at least early, against a Rutgers team that has used that formula successfully this season. Oddly this game could more closely resemble, say, the Louisville game than some of the Boilermakers’ non-conference home matchups.

2-As such rebounding will be key. It’s no secret Purdue has, at times, been exposed via second-chance points. Rutgers has been as good as anyone at getting on the offensive boards. Vincent Edwards will need some help.

3-Right now you could probably make a case for three Boilermakers as first team All-Big Ten: Carsen Edwards, Vincent Edwards and Dakota Mathias. Isaac Haas had a dominant first weekend of Big Ten play and P.J. Thompson is one of the league’s most underrated players. It’s a minor note in the overall picture of a season but the next 16 games provide a chance for individuals to separate from their league peers with their production.

4-Rutgers has not proven it can shoot with any measure of consistency. So how will they generate an offense tonight if Matt Haarms and Haas also create problems in the lane and at the rim? This is where clean offense – not giving transition points away via turnover – helps the Boilermaker defense.

5-I know some fans are looking sideways at much of the rest of the Big Ten. But if you think trips to Ann Arbor and Minneapolis next week are easy you’re kidding yourself. A sense of urgency against Rutgers and Nebraska this week – regardless of their Ken Pom ratings or bracketology expectations – is essential in a championship chase.

Dec. 30: Pregame vs. Lipscomb

Purdue basketball closes its non-conference schedule tonight against Lipscomb at Mackey Arena. BTN will broadcast the 8 p.m. tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue brings an eight-game winning streak but has not played since last Thursday’s 97-48 victory over Tennessee State.

Lipscomb has won three straight but also has not played since last Thursday, a 73-64 win at Navy.

Purdue opens Big Ten Conference play on Wednesday with a home game against Rutgers.

STARTING FIVE

1-Purdue can’t afford to overlook Lipscomb, and it sounds like the coaching staff hammered that point home this week. This Bisons have no KenPom wins above No. 87 (two against Belmont). However, they’ve played at Alabama, Tennessee and Texas. I’d be surprised if this was a team intimidated by the environment.

2-More importantly, Lipscomb is ranked just outside the NCAA’s RPI top 25. This isn’t just a “win and move on” non-conference buy game. Purdue can extra more tangible value from this game than most of its home games to date.

3-If Lipscomb makes an upset bid, it may hinge on two areas: rebounding and 3-point defense. Those can work in tandem, obviously. Purdue hasn’t really turned a corner on the boards and must get better there throughout Big Ten play.

4-It will be interesting to see how Lipscomb tries to negate Purdue’s 7-footers consider it has almost literally no size. Can the Boilermakers’ perimeter defenders force Lipscomb to try to score over Haas and Haarms?

5-On top of that, Lipscomb has only two players shooting above 30 percent from 3-point range. We’ve seen players without a perimeter reputation mysteriously got hot against the Boilermakers before.

Dec. 21: Pregame vs. Tennessee State

No. 16 Purdue plays the penultimate game of its non-conference schedule tonight when Tennessee State visits Mackey Arena. The 6 p.m. tipoff will be broadcast on BTN.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue has won seven straight games, including Saturday’s 82-67 victory over Butler in the Crossroads Classic. After tonight’s game, the Boilermakers conclude the non-conference with a Dec. 30 game against Lipscomb.

Tennessee State is 5-5 but is coming off a 47-46 loss at Texas on Monday.

Purdue announced Thursday morning that it has dismissed forward Eden Ewing for a violation of team rules. On the court this won’t make much of an impact because Ewing had not earned rotation minutes.

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Boilers establishing free throw edge with veteran savvy

EDEN EWING: Purdue basketball dismisses forward for violation of team rules

CARSEN EDWARDS: Purdue sophomore trending up as midseason approaches

STARTING FIVE

1-The usual “just win” schpeel obviously applies here. Purdue is two games away from surviving the non-conference “buy” games without a major stumble.

2-Turnover have come and gone as a potential problem for Purdue. Tennessee State’s most effective weapon is its ability to turn teams over. We’ll see how the Boilermakers handle that.

3-Playing three games in a 24-day span obviously doesn’t leave much time for getting in game situations. Purdue will address that in practice, but nothing substitutes for the real thing, obviously.

4-Carsen Edwards has put together a couple of solid performances back-to-back. It would be a positive development for Purdue if he can carry that momentum forward into the new year.

5-That’s all for tonight. Merry Christmas everyone.

Dec. 16: Pregame vs. Butler in Crossroads Classic

INDIANAPOLIS ― No. 17 Purdue goes for its second Crossroads Classic victory in as many seasons when it plays Butler today at Bankers Life Fieldhouse. The noon tipoff will be broadcast by FOX.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue has won six straight, including last Sunday’s 86-61 victory over IUPUI.

Butler has won five straight, most recently a 95-67 victory over Youngstown State last Saturday.

Butler announced that guard Sean McDermott is dressed and available to play today. The sharpshooter was originally ecxpected to miss 4-6 weeks after suffering an ankle injury on Nov. 24.

Purdue junior guard Ryan Cline missed Thursday’s practice due to the flu, but practiced Friday and is expected to play today. He has made 8 of 12 from 3-point range over his last three games.

Purdue was 0-5 in the Crossroads Classic before defeating Notre Dame last season. Butler is 5-1, with three of those victories coming over Purdue.

CROSSROADS CLASSIC: Purdue wants to reverse another trend against Butler

STARTING FIVE

1-Coming back from a turnover-fueled deficit against IUPUI is one thing. Purdue can’t afford a similar stumble out of the gates against Butler.

2-As always it will be interesting to see how the Bulldogs handle the post against Purdue. Butler can foul with its abundance of centers, but Isaac Haas is shooting north of 80 percent at the line this season. The key will be how well Butler limits the Boilermakers’ attempts to get the ball on the block in the first place.

3-Key defensive matchups for Purdue: Kelan Martin is averaging 20.8 points and 8.2 rebounds during Butler’s five-game winning streak. Paul Jorgensen has made 16 of 30 from 3-point range.

4-PJ Thompson has 13 assists against one turnover over his past two Crossroads Classic games. In general ball security will be important against a Butler team with a plus-four turnover ratio.

5-This is Purdue’s last big test of the non-conference and its biggest obstacle to taking a nine-game winning streak into the resumption of Big Ten play. From my conversations earlier this week it appeared the Boilermakers recognized the opportunity Saturday’s game presents. Much like the trip to Marquette, this is one of those games that demands a mature performance.

Dec. 7: Pregame vs. Valparaiso

Purdue basketball plays the first of three straight non-conference in-state matchups tonight against Valparaiso. The 6:30 p.m. tipoff airs on FS1.

For updates and analysis, follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

No. 21 Purdue has won four straight games, including a 74-69 victory over Northwestern last Sunday. The Boilermakers opened the first weekend of Big Ten Conference play 2-0.

Valparaiso is 8-0. The Crusaders haven’t played since a 72-65 home victory over Utah State on Nov. 28.

Purdue next plays IUPUI at home on Sunday and against Butler in the Crossroads Classic at Bankers Life Fieldhouse on Dec. 16.

VINCENT EDWARDS: How the Purdue senior regained his old rebounding form

ISAAC HAAS: Purdue senior named Big Ten Player of the Week

STARTING FIVE

1-Dakota Mathias confessed the Boilermakers heard a lot about Ball State in the 24 hours or so after the Cardinals upset Notre Dame earlier this week. He also said that wasn’t necessary because Purdue already received its wake-up call, from Western Kentucky. Between now and the resumption of Big Ten play on Jan. 3, Purdue will face a handful of these heavily favored matchups. In theory, it’s where a veteran team should thrive.

2-Valparaiso thrives on defense but hasn’t played anyone listed higher than 133rd in the KenPom.com rankings. The Boilermakers will test that defense in new ways. Presumably the Crusaders will defend the post one-on-one with their own 7-footers. Could that lead to another big game for Isaac Haas? He ranks 12th nationally in fouls drawn per 40 minutes – a trend Valpo’s big men must counter.

3-Since blowing up against Arizona, Carsen Edwards has made 2 of his last 15 from 3-point range. He’s due to lock in for a night or at least a long stretch.

4-Valpo’s a solid offensive rebounding team. Purdue has, at times, been exposed on second chances. Something to keep an eye on tonight.

5-To spring these upsets teams typically need a star player to step up. The Crusader to watch tonight is Tevonn Walker. The senior knows how to get to the free throw line and create offense through his defense.

Dec. 3: Pregame vs. Northwestern

Purdue celebrates the 50th anniversary of Mackey Arena and wraps up the first weekend of Big Ten Conference play today against Northwestern. The 4 p.m. tipoff will be broadcast on BTN.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue sports reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue opened Big Ten play on Friday with an 80-75 victory at Maryland. The Boilermakers have won three straight games.

PURDUE 80, MARYLAND 75: Haas leads defending champion Purdue to Big Ten-opening win

Northwestern opened with a 72-68 overtime home victory over Illinois on Friday.

Sunday’s game is Purdue’s sixth in 12 days.

On a personal note, it won't be the same today without seeing Jeff Washburn before, during and after the game. He would have loved nothing more than to be courtside today, covering the celebration of a building that meant a lot to him and this community. Thanks for everything, Wash.

STARTING FIVE

1-It’s a fair question: After recovering to win three straight, does Purdue have to win today to call this six-game stretch a success? I think, in many ways, it does. No room for taking a step back right now and the way Northwestern is playing, that’s what this would be called. Being able to bank a 2-0 Big Ten start for January would be big.

2-Unlike some of the Boilermakers’ recent opponents, Northwestern doesn’t pose the same athletic threat. That’s why Purdue can’t give up a bunch of second-chance points or turn the ball over in the backcourt. Louisville and Maryland both had several second-chance opportunities on which they didn’t capitalize. Did Purdue dodge one, or will it catch up today?

3-Purdue has never had much success shutting down Bryant McIntosh. No shame there – he’s really good. A potentially bigger matchup is Scottie Lindsey, since at 6-5 he can cause some matchup problems. Mathias may take that assignment in certain lineup configurations, but this seems like a big moment for Carsen Edwards.

4-I assume Northwestern will try to double Isaac Haas today. The Wildcats simply don’t have much size. Could be a big day for Purdue shooters in their home environs.

5-Speaking of, will the Boilermakers feed off the energy of the 50th anniversary celebration for Mackey Arena? The 1969 Final Four is expected to be in attendance, among the presentations.

Defending Big Ten Conference champion Purdue opens conference play tonight at Maryland. BTN will broadcast the 7 p.m. tipoff.

For updates and analysis follow Journal & Courier Purdue sports reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue comes into the opening weekend of league play off of back-to-back victories. It defeated Louisville 66-57 on Tuesday in a Big Ten/ACC Challenge game.

Maryland lost to Syracuse in its Big Ten/ACC Challenge game for its second loss in three games.

Purdue returns home Sunday to play Northwestern. No other Big Ten games will be played until after Jan. 1.

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Maryland

BIG WEEKEND: Purdue begins Big Ten Conference title defense

ISAAC HAAS: Purdue center ready after scary moment against Louisville

STARTING FIVE

1-I imagine Maryland is pretty motivated to avenge that weird last-second loss last season. (Weird because of that Isaac Haas game-ending interception/travel, not how Purdue won, really.) For the Boilermakers this is a chance to affirm the progress it showed against Arizona and Louisville.

2-PJ Thompson vs Anthony Cowan looms as a big matchup. Can the Boilermakers defend the Maryland PG without sending him to the foul line? (He gets there seven-plus times per game.) That’s also a potentially tough assignment for Nojel Eastern in reserve, but maybe his size can create some unique problems for Cowan.

3-Purdue has to assert itself on the boards. There’s no better way to get beat on the road than to get buried by second-chance points. Who steps up and helps Vincent Edwards?

4-Another defensive matchup to watch is Dakota Mathias vs. (presumably) Kevin Huerter. The 6-7 wing just hit seven 3-pointers against Syracuse, but his offensive game has a wider profile than that.

5-Even if Haas starts tonight, we may see more of Matt Haarms, depending on any lingering injury effects for the senior center. Especially late in games, Haarms’ defensive abilities seem critical to have on the floor.

Nov. 28: Pregame vs. Louisville in Big Ten/ACC Challenge

Purdue basketball returns to Mackey Arena to face No. 17 Louisville as part of the Big Ten/ACC Challenge series.

Tipoff is scheduled for 8 p.m. on ESPN.

For updates and analysis, follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue is coming off a 1-2 performance at the Battle 4 Atlantis. The Boilermakers lost to Tennessee and Western Kentucky before defeating Arizona in the seventh-place game.

Purdue subsequently dropped out of the Associated Press and USA Today coaches polls.

The Boilermakers traveled to Louisville in last year’s Big Ten/ACC Challenge, losing 71-64.

PURDUE VS. LOUISVILLE: Boilermakers must quickly apply lessons of Atlantis

BATTLE 4 ATLANTIS: What we learned from Purdue basketball in the Bahamas

STARTING FIVE

1-Set the result aside, because Purdue could play well tonight and still lose to a very solid team. It’s equally important for the Boilermakers to start strong and show some resiliency in the possible if not likely occurrence that Louisville’s defense creates some problems. Purdue can’t match Louisville’s athleticism, so it needs to maximize potential advantages in skill and, less tangibly, poise. Getting back in front of a home crowd probably won’t hurt, either.

2-A year ago, Louisville shut down Purdue’s interior scoring game until well into the second half. If and when the Cardinals take away Isaac Haas and Matt Haarms, how will the Boilermakers respond? (It should help that Purdue is cranking in 51.3 percent of its 3-pointers at home.)

3-Vincent Edwards has never averaged more than 5.4 rebounds in a reason. He’s averaging 9.0 right now, pulling down eight or more in five of seven games. Louisville hasn’t had great success on the offensive boards yet, and Purdue needs to make sure it doesn’t start tonight. The Boilermakers rank only 10th among Big Ten Conference teams in defensive rebound percentage.

4-The Boilermakers turned the ball over 17 times against Louisville last season. They just averaged 15 per game at the Battle 4 Atlantis. Considering Purdue won’t usually dominate other good teams on the boards, those turnover numbers need to come down. (Of note: the Cardinals’ last two opponents turned the ball over 35 times combined.) Can Carsen Edwards repeat his efficient performance against Arizona?

5-Louisville is one of the best shot-blocking teams in the nation. Can Matt Haarms be an equalizer here? His 3.0 per game rank third among all Big Ten players in only 17 minutes per game. Situationally, can Jacquil Taylor also be a defensive asset in a matchup like this against a lengthy, athletic opponent?

Nov. 27: Associated Press poll streak ends

Purdue’s 41-week stay in the Associated Press rankings ended Monday when the Boilermakers dropped out of the top 25.

Purdue had been ranked in every poll since the 2015-16 preseason poll.

Purdue was ranked No. 18 last week before going 1-2 at the Battle 4 Atlantis. It lost to unranked Tennessee and Western Kentucky before defeating then-No. 2 Arizona in the seventh-place game. The Wildcats went 0-3 and also dropped out of the top 25.

Purdue ranks fourth among teams “also receiving votes,” one spot ahead of Arizona.

Duke remained No. 1. Michigan State is No. 3 and Minnesota is No. 12.

Purdue also dropped out of the USA Today coaches poll, where it had been ranked 16th. It is the top team among those also receiving votes, 10 points behind No. 25 UCLA.

• Junior guard Ryan Cline showed up for pre-practice interviews today with a mat in hand. He said he’d been suffering back spasms since tweaking his back during weightlifting on Sunday. Cline said he only practiced about 20 minutes that day before shutting down.

However, Cline expected to practice today and said he expects to play Tuesday against Louisville.

UPDATE: Cline did practice Monday, so barring any next-day issues he's expected to play Tuesday.

• Painter said no further decision has been made on the potential redshirt status of Aaron Wheeler. Painter said after the season opener that the freshman forward would probably use a redshirt season but a final decision had not been made.

"We're still evaluating and going from there and seeing where we are with our team," Painter said.

Grady Eifert has been the primary option behind Vincent Edwards. Jacquil Taylor also played a handful of minutes at the 4 against Arizona.

Nov. 24: Pregame vs. Arizona in the Battle 4 Atlantis

PARADISE ISLAND, Bahamas — Purdue basketball tries to avoid an 0-3 trip to tbe Battle 4 Atlantis when it faces No. 4 Arizona in the seventh-place game.

The 9:30 p.m. tipoff will not be broadcast but is available via ESPN3 webstream.

For updates and analysis during the game, follow Journal & Courier reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue lost to Western Kentucky 77-73 on Thursday night for its second loss in as many games. The Boilermakers led for only 44 seconds. P.J. Thompson hit 5 of 10 3s and scored 19 points, but the rest of the team went 3-for-17 behind the arc.

Arizona lost to SMU 66-60 in the other consolation semifinal. The Wildcats came into the tournament ranked No. 2 in the AP poll and No. 4 by the coaches.

WESTERN KENTUCKY 77, PURDUE 73: Boilermakers sputter in loss to WKU

STARTING FIVE

1-Before he even knew who his team was playing, Purdue coach Matt Painter delivered a message. “If you're going to feel sorry for yourself, you're going to lose another one.” The Boilermakers seemed wounded after the first two losses, but it can’t afford to lick those wounds tonight. Arizona’s own psyche might be affected right now by personnel absences, the lingering FBI investigation and who knows what else. But you can’t deny the Wildcats’ talent.

2-We don’t know where Tennessee and Western Kentucky will be at the end of the season. (The Volunteers beat N.C. State today to finsh third.) As I said before, either of those losses by itself doesn’t impact a season that much. But the missed opportunity of an 0-3 run in Atlantis will almost surely impact Purdue’s NCAA Tournament future. It doesn’t mean big goals aren’t still attainable, but they’ll be harder to achieve. On the other hand, beating even a similarly struggling Arizona team tonight will almost surely pay dividends in March – not to mention the residual benefit of a confidence boost going into next week’s tough schedule.

3-Tonight is pretty clearly a gut-check moment for Purdue. Regardless of strategies and matchups, do the Boilermakers really want to leave the island with the stigma of losing the toughness battle three straight nights? Arizona may have more to lose, in theory, but not by much. Purdue has a lot to prove to itself tonight.

4-Purdue’s jump shooting issues sorta don’t make sense. Did the players respond well when shots didn’t fall vs. WKU? No. If even one of the 19 missed 3s (many of which were very clean looks) goes in does it perhaps flip the outcome? Probably. While the Boilermakers need to make better decisions, they must maintain their confident approach when ball movement creates half-court opportunities.

5-Some have asked about Jacquil Taylor’s absence in light of Purdue’s rebounding problems. To my knowledge there is no additional injury situation with the junior center, except he is so recently back from missing nearly a month. But Taylor also has offensive limitations compared to Haas and Haarms and there’s no evidence he’d be better in other capacities, either. Haas and Haarms are the better players and simply need to play like it.

Nov. 23: Pregame vs. Western Kentucky

No. 16 Purdue basketball plays Western Kentucky in a consolation semifinal at the Battle 4 Atlantis. The 7 p.m. tipoff will not be broadcast but is available via ESPN3 webcast or the Watch ESPN app.

Follow Journal & Courier reporter Nathan Baird during the game for updates and analysis on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Tennessee defeated Purdue 78-75 in overtime in Wednesday’s tournament opener. The Boilermakers committed turnovers and were outscored 21-11 on second-chance points as Tennessee grabbed 20 offensive rebounds. Purdue couldn’t hold on to a five-point lead in overtime.

The loss seemed even more significant at the time because it likely denied Purdue a chance to play either of the top-five teams at the event. However, N.C. State later upset No. 4 Arizona, meaning the Boilermakers could face the Wildcats in Friday’s fifth-place game if they beat Western Kentucky. Arizona plays SMU, another team in the KenPom top 25, in the other consolation semifinal.

TENNESSEE 78, PURDUE 75 (OT): Boilermakers fall in Battle 4 Atlantis opener

BATTLE 4 ATLANTIS: Purdue's new mission is to find identity after opening loss

BATTLE 4 ATLANTIS: A look at the talented field in this year's event

STARTING FIVE

1-Will Purdue’s loss to Tennessee look better in time? (The Volunteers led Villanova by 12 at halftime before fading.) Are matchups wit SMU and/or Arizona less desirable now that those teams were also upset? Maybe. Taking nothing away from Tennessee, Purdue simply didn’t play well in several facets Wednesday. Tonight’s game has a gut-check feel to it.

2-Isaac Haas finished with three rebounds in 24 minutes, which is an issue. Purdue also wasn’t able to utilize him on the offensive end enough (though his four turnovers contributed also). He’ll have a height advantage today, but WKU has some veteran big men who have seen those mismatches before. How will he, and Matt Haarms, respond?

3-Purdue collectively needs to run its offense better today. I think Tennessee was more disruptive to that than the Boilermakers seemed to in postgame interviews. But quick perimeter shots definitely hurt Purdue’s flow. In particular, Carsen Edwards could use a bounce-back performance.

4-Vincent Edwards had 13 rebounds Wednesday and continues to excel there for Purdue. It’s sure starting to seem like he can sustain that increased production on the boards.

5-Purdue should have an advantage again today in crowd support. Do things things matter in neutral court scenarios? I didn’t make a difference Wednesday, but perhaps it can provide a needed emotional boost.

Nov. 22: Pregame vs. Tennessee

No. 16 Purdue basketball opens Battle 4 Atlantis play against Tennessee. The noon tipoff will be broadcast on ESPN2.

For updates and analysis during the game, follow Journal & Courier reporter Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue seeks its third straight in-season tournament championship after winning the 2015 Naismith Memorial Hall of Fame Tip-Off and the 2016 Cancun Challenge. However, the field at the Battle 4 Atlantis incudes a pair of top-five teams in Villanova and Arizona.

The Boilermakers are 4-0 after defeating Fairfield 106-64. Purdue has scored 100 or more points in three of those games.

Tennessee is 2-0 after easy wins over Presbyterian and High Point.

BATTLE 4 ATLANTIS: Purdue begins pivotal early stretch in the Bahamas

STARTING FIVE

1-Goes without saying but a stretch that could help define Purdue’s season all but comes undone with a loss here. Western Kentucky brought only seven active scholarship players to the Bahamas. Neither Northern Iowa nor North Carolina State projects as an NCAA Tournament resume-building opponent. Those are the teams the Boilermakers would face if they fall into the consolation bracket.

2-On the other hand, Purdue has pretty well taken care of business this season, with the exception of the end of the first half at Marquette. Recent history suggests this is a team that will come out focused on Tennessee. The Volunteers have gone on runs of 28-0 and 32-0 over the first two games. While Purdue doesn’t seem susceptible to such a skid, Tennessee can’t be taken lightly.

3-Relative to the rest of the Big Ten Conference, Purdue remains a very adequate rebounding team. Not great, but not poor, either. Considering the way this team is playing offensively, middle-of-the-pack might be enough.

4-Admiral Schofield isn't just an aptly named player for a tournament being played on an island nation. He could be an intriguing defensive matchup for Purdue as a solid body with some perimeter range. I’m also eager to see James Daniel after missing him when Howard visited Mackey Arena (DNP, toe).

5-You know who’s due? Ryan Cline is due. He’s contributing in other areas, but that early 2-for-12 clip from 3-point range can’t last. Maybe he’ll take to the shooting backdrop in the ballroom here at Atlantis.

Nov. 18: Pregame vs. Fairfield

No. 21 Purdue basketball plays its final warm-up prior to the Battle 4 Atlantis today against Fairfield.

Tipoff is set for 7 p.m. The game is available via web streaming on BTN Plus.

For updates and analysis during the game, follow Journal & Courier’s Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue improved to 3-0 with an 86-71 victory at Marquette on Tuesday. Isaac Haas scored 14 of his 22 points in the second half as the Boilermakers pulled away from a two-point halftime lead.

PURDUE 86, MARQUETTE 71: Boilermakers' maturity showed up in convincing victory

Purdue begins play at the Battle 4 Atlantis on Wednesday against Tennessee. Other teams in the field include No. 5 Arizona, No. 6 Villanova and SMU.

STARTING FIVE

1-Not sure what Purdue will do on defensive matchups but one likely pairing is Dakota Mathias on Fairfield guard Tyler Nelson. He’s a 20 points-per-game guy who can dial it up from deep.

2-A corollary to No. 1, the Stags haven’t shot the ball well early (22.5 percent from 3-point range). Purdue opponents are under 30 percent for the young season despite Marquette’s hot streak on Tuesday.

3-Vincent Edwards is averaging 15.6 rebounds per 40 minutes. That’s easily Purdue’s best rate right now, ahead of Isaac Haas’ 12.4. Edwards told me he wants 10 per night, which he almost got against Marquette despite playing only 16 minutes. Does Fairfield have enough size on the front line to slow him up?

4-Purdue needs to look cleaner on offense after turning it over eight times in the first half against Marquette. That’s the kind of start that would capsize an upset bid at the Battle 4 Atlantis. Keep an eye on how Purdue starts today.

5-Purdue can turn some heads in the Bahamas next week. So the main goals today should be to wrap things up early and keep the rotation healthy.

Nov. 16: Boilermakers in 2018 Gildan Charleston Classic

Purdue is part of the eight-team field for the 2018 Gildan Charleston Classic. The exempt tournament will be held Nov. 15-18 at TD Arena in Charleston. S.C.

Wichita State, Alabama and Virginia Tech are the other major-conference schools in the field. Appalachian State, Ball State, Davidson and Northeastern complete the entries.

The event is owned and operated by ESPN.

Purdue will play in the Battle 4 Atlantis in the Bahamas next week. The Boilermakers seek their third straight in-season tournament championship following the Cancun Challenge (2016) and the Naismith Memorial Hall of Fame Tipoff (2015).

Nov. 15: Vincent Edwards on Wooden Award watch list

Purdue senior forward Vincent Edwards is one of 50 players on the Wooden Award preseason watch list.

The award, named for the former Purdue All-American, is presented annually to the national player of the year.

This is the third watch list mention for Edwards, who is also a preseason candidate for the Naismith Award and the Erving Award. (More info on both below).

Nov. 14: Pregame vs. Marquette in Gavitt Games

No. 21 Purdue basketball goes for a 3-0 start to the season tonight at Marquette as part of the Gavitt Games. Tipoff is scheduled for 8:30 p.m. on FS1.

For updates and analysis during the game follow Journal & Courier Purdue basketball insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue opened the season with victories over SIU-Edwardsville and Chicago State. Marquette will present a tougher test, as detailed in our scouting report:

SCOUTING REPORT: Purdue basketball at Marquette

The Boilermakers return to the BMO Harris Bradley Center. Purdue defeated Vermont and Iowa State here in NCAA Tournament Midwest Region first and second-round games last March.

The Gavitt Games is an annual series between the Big Ten and Big East conferences named for former Big East commissioner Dave Gavitt. Purdue lost to Villanova at Mackey Arena last season in its first participation in the event.

Purdue moved up to No. 19 in the Associated Press poll on Monday. As far as I can tell no updated coaches poll has been released, hence the No. 21 used above.

STARTING FIVE

1-Rick Mount hit one of the most memorable shots in Purdue history the last time these two teams met – in a 1969 NCAA Tournament Regional final. Fittingly, tonight’s matchup features two of the most prolific shooting teams around. Marquette hasn’t clicked from the perimeter yet, but based on their players’ past performance, it could happen any moment. Purdue must be ready to contest shots and avoid transition scenarios in which it can’t set its defense.

2-Marquette was an NCAA team last season. The importance of quality road victories in terms of one’s NCAA Tournament profile can’t be overstated. Even if the Golden Eagles take a step back this season, tonight presents a good opportunity for Purdue to secure a solid early victory after two cakewalks. While more opportunities abound next week at the Battle 4 Atlantis, the matchups there are somewhat out of the Boilermakers’ hands.

3-As a corollary to the above, Purdue should conceivably be a pretty good road team this season. To begin with take four senior starters, a fourth-year junior in Jacquil Taylor and young players who’ve already played in several big games. Now take newcomers who spent a summer playing in unique and unfamiliar venues. Poise would seem to be a strength, but those questions are typically answered one adverse in-game situation at a time.

4-Purdue is playing its third game in five days, but its starters did not need to play deep into either of the first two. Fatigue shouldn’t be an issue. Two players to keep an eye on are Isaac Haas and Jacquil Taylor (assuming he plays). Haas potential this season hinges on his ability to not only stay on the floor, but remain productive when he has formerly been prone to fouls and turnovers. I’m not telling him anything he doesn’t know (and he wouldn’t read this anyway). But it might be something he grows into early.

5-Dakota Mathias has been fantastic through two games. I don’t know if he’ll ever get the chance to prove he can guard NBA-level 2s. If he does, however, someone might decide he belongs on a roster. He can clearly shoot at that level, and he plays such a clean game. I’ll have more on this next week but some Big Ten coaches think he’s Purdue’s best player.

Nov. 12: Pregame vs. Chicago State

No. 21 Purdue plays the second of three games in five days today when it plays Chicago State at Mackey Arena. The 4 p.m. tipoff will be broadcast by BTN.

For updates and analysis during the game, follow Journal & Courier insider Nathan Baird on Twitter: @nbairdjc

Purdue opened the season with a 105-74 victory over SIU-Edwardsville on Friday. Dakota Mathias scored 18 points to lead five players in double figures.

The Boilermakers wore shooting shirts of their own design while warming up prior to that game. The team said it wanted to use its stage to send a message of unity and positive ideals.

Purdue travels to Marquette on Tuesday as part of the annual Gavitt Games series between the Big Ten Conference and the Big East.

STARTING FIVE

1-Is Purdue still just a bit behind on rebounding, or is this just not an especially good rebounding team? Too soon to know, and a better performance today won’t answer the question completely.

2-Purdue coach Matt Painter said the brief time Isaac Haas and Matt Haarms played together on Friday had more to do with getting both of them minutes than anything. However, he admitted Haarms’ mobility makes such a pairing possible. If and when it happens again, it probably won’t be for a long stretch, and the offensive production must be significant to make up for what Purdue gives up at the other end.

3-Jacquil Taylor played eight minutes in the opener. He’s being eased back and looked a bit rusty at times, as should be expected after missing the better part of month. Again, long-term health is more important at this juncture.

4-Purude blocked eight shots Friday, which is good. It also still suffered from some dribble penetration leakage early, which is not. Besides rebounding that’s the other phase most in need of improvement.

5-Nojel Eastern became only the fourth Purdue freshman to post eight assists in a game, and he did so in only 21 minutes. They came with only two turnovers. It’s only one example of the progress he’s made since the summer.

Nov. 9: Vincent Edwards on Naismith Award watch list

Purdue senior forward Vincent Edwards added another preseason honor Thursday when he was named to the watch list for the Naismith Award.

He is one of 50 players watch listed for the award, given annually to the national player of the year.

Edwards, the only returning player in the nation with 1,000 points, 500 rebounds and 300 assists, is also on the Julius Erving Award watch list.

Other Big Ten Conference players on the watch list include Michigan State’s Miles Bridge and Nick Wards, Wisconsin’s Ethan Happ, Maryland’s Justin Jackson, Minnesota’s Nate Mason, Northwestern’s Bryant McIntosh and Michigan’s Moritz Wagner.

Players on or potentially on Purdue's schedule include Louisville's Deng Adel, Arizona's Rawle Alkins, Deandre Ayton and Allonzo Trier, Villanova's Jalen Brunson, Butler's Kelan Martin and SMU's Shake Milton.

This is the 50th year of the Naismith Award. Purdue’s Glenn Robinson won in 1994.

Nov. 5: Boilermakers win closed scrimmage with West Virginia

I obviously wasn’t at Purdue’s closed — or if you prefer, “secret” — scrimmage against West Virginia in Columbus, Ohio, on Sunday.

I linked above to an article I wrote in 2015 about the hush-hush nature of the scrimmages. Back then any publicity of the scrimmages was verboten. The veil of secrecy has been removed to the point that Purdue tweeted out a highlight video from today:

No one released a box score or a final score, but here's a bit of what I heard from some who were there:

• Purdue won easily, pulling away in the second half. Comfortably stayed up by double-digits in the second half.

• I was most curious how the turnovers were go. It was an issue last season when Purdue scrimmaged West Virginia, which made some sense. Matt Painter wanted the Mountaineers’ high-energy press to challenge his team.

I'm told Purdue committed 16 turnovers Sunday. Not a horrible number, perhaps especially considering it committed 12 in the first half against NAIA Carroll College last Wednesday. Some of the Boilermakers’ turnovers in recent years came via its extreme priority on playing through the post. That’s still a priority, but not as much, and as a result the turnover percentages should (in theory) improve. We’ll see.

• I'm also told Purdue outrebounded West Virginia by six. Obviously, another area of emphasis early this season. West Virginia has some good wings and forwards but no Isaac Haas or Matt Haarms-like size. (They do have a couple of 6-10, 6-11 sophomores.)

• Sounds like the wings and guards played pretty well. I’m told the “normal rotation” played. That did not include Jacquil Taylor, who Painter said likely would not return to practice until Tuesday.

Purdue needed a solid performance to end the preseason, and it sounds like this qualifies in most areas. West Virginia opens the season ranked 10th in the USA Today coaches poll and seventh in the KenPom rankings. This is the third straight year in which Purdue has competed in a closed scrimmage, starting with Dayton in 2015.

The Boilermakers open the season this weekend against SIU-Edwardsville (Friday) and Chicago State (Sunday). However, that Nov. 14 trip to Milwaukee is right around the corner, and a week later, Purdue is in the Bahamas.

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Boilermakers will again scrimmage West Virginia

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Preseason player profile capsules

Nov. 1: Boilermakers open season No. 20 in Associated Press poll

Purdue opens the season No. 20 in the first Associated Press men's basketball poll, released Wednesday.

The Boilermakers are ranked fifth among Big Ten Conference teams, behind No. 2 Michigan State, No. 15 Minnesota and No. 19 Northwestern.

Among other Purdue opponents, Louisville is ranked No. 16. Two teams in the Battle 4 Atlantis field — No. 3 Arizona and No. 6 Villanova — could play the Boilermakers during that Thanksgiving week tournament.

Purdue will participate in a closed scrimmage against No. 11 West Virginia on Sunday.

Duke received 33 of 65 first-place votes and is ranked No. 1. Michigan State (13), Arizona (18) and No. 4 Kansas (1) received the other No. 1 votes.

Last month, Purdue was ranked No. 21 in the first USA Today Coaches poll.

• Per Purdue basketball sports information director Chris Forman, Jacquil Taylor received a positive result from Monday's CT scan on his left foot. While the senior center previously hoped to return for Sunday's closed scrimmage against West Virginia, Forman said it's more likely Taylor returns for the season opener against SIU-Edwardsville on Nov. 10.

(Obviously, Taylor will not play in Wednesday's exhibition against Carroll College of Montana.)

Taylor has been held out since early October for precautionary reasons. A previous CT scan showed a possible complication in the junior center's recovery from ankle surgery that cost him all of last season.

Taylor played well this summer in his first action back from that surgery. His presence as a defender and rebounder, in particular, would enhance a post situation that includes senior center Isaac Haas and redshirt freshman Matt Haarms.

Taylor spoke yesterday of how he's tried to stay positive through the injury uncertainty:

• Purdue opens the season ranked fourth in ESPN's Basketball Power Index. Per ESPN, the rating is meant to represent "how many points above or below average a team is." Strength of schedule — or more accurately, strength of record — is a major component.

Wichita State, Villanova and Kansas are the teams ranked ahead of Purdue. Big Ten teams in the top 25 include Michigan State (13) and Minnesota (21).

Oct. 20: Isaac Haas on Kareem Abdul-Jabbar Award watch list

Purdue coach Matt Painter said Thursday that Isaac Haas will be a go-to player for the first time this season.

That could also mean a brighter national spotlight for the 7-2, 290-pound center. On Friday he was named as one of 20 watch list candidates for the Kareem Abdul-Jabbar Award, given annually to the nation's best center.

Haas averaged 12.6 points. 5 rebounds and 0.7 blocks last season while shooting 58.7 percent from the field. He led the Big Ten Conference in points per 40 minutes for the second straight season.

After two years playing behind and/or alongside A.J. Hammons and Caleb Swanigan, Haas goes into his senior season as Purdue's primary weapon in the post.

Wisconsin's Ethan Happ is the only other Big Ten Conference player on the watch list. Arizona's Dusan Ristic and N.C. State's Omer Yurtseven are both in the Battle 4 Atlantis field with the Boilermakers.

Wisconsin's Frank Kaminsky won the inagural award in 2015.

Oct. 18: Vincent Edwards makes Erving Award watch list

For the second straight season, senior forward Vincent Edwards made the watch list for the Julius Erving Award.

The award, given annually to the nation's top small forward, enters its fourth season. (Technically Edwards doesn't play the 3 in Purdue's lineups, but it's the position that in some ways most closely resembles his skiil set.)

The 6-8, 225-pound Edwards is the nation's only active player with 1,000 points, 500 rebounds and 300 assists. He averaged 12.7 points last season.

Michigan State's Miles Bridges and Maryland's Justin Jackson also made the 20-player watch list.

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Vincent Edwards ready for central role

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Preseason player profile capsules

Oct. 19: Media picks Boilermakers second in preseason poll

The Big Ten Conference media does not expect Purdue to repeat as league champion this season.

Not even close, actually. A panel of 28 media members unanimously picked Michigan State to wear the crown in the coming season.

Full disclosure: I was one of them, and you can read my full ballot here:

BIG TEN BASKTBALL: Nathan Baird's preseason ballots

Brendan Quinn of The Athletic Detroit and Adam Jardy of the Columbus Dispatch teamed up to resurrect the media poll. Click here to view the full poll results, as well as a look back at the poll's history.

The consensus of the votes picked Purdue as the league's runner-up, ahead of Minnesota and Northwestern.

Michigan State's Miles Bridges was the unanimous preseason Player of the Year choice. Spartans freshman Jaren Jackson received all but one vote for preseason Freshman of the Year.

Preseason first team All-Big Ten honors went to Bridges, Wisconsin's Ethan Happ (both unanimous), Michigan's Mo Wagner, Minnesota's Nate Mason and Northwestern's Bryant McIntosh.

Purdue's Vincent Edwards was the next-highest vote-getter, and the only other player to receive more than two votes (he received 12).

Sept. 28: Could Rapheal Davis return to Purdue?

Rapheal Davis joined us this week on the BoilersXTRA Podcast (below). We chatted with the former Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year about his return to campus for homecoming weekend, his surprise Big Ten championship ring presentation and his thoughts on the Boilermaker basketball newcomers.

I also talked to Davis about his basketball future. Matt Painter approached Davis about a graduate assistant position in the offseason. Davis, however, had just started a job in pharmaceutical sales in Georgia and didn't feel the timing was right.

Davis said he told Painter to let him know when such a position opens in the future. Few players in recent Boilermaker history were held in higher regard by the coaching staff for their work ethic and leadership. Also known for his dedication to film study, he would seem to be a perfect fit for such a position — if he has decided to pursue a coaching career. As he discussed on the podcast, Davis is helping coach at the high school and AAU levels in Georgia.

Davis called returning to Purdue "my ultimate dream."

"I'll tell you this — whether it be a coach, whether it be GA, whether it be film guy, whether it be something, one day I will be running through that tunnel again with the guys," Davis said. "I may not be in uniform. I may be in a suit. But I can definitely see that in my future."

• One scholarship remains open for the coming season. That may eventually go to Grady Eifert, the walk-on who played on scholarship last season and, coincidentally, had a really nice summer.

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Grady Eifert seizing unexpected opportunity

However, that may not happen until the second semester. Regardless of the ramifications of the still-unfolding corruption scandal, the transfer culture across the sport is such that teams need to keep their options open.

Specifically as it pertains to this roster, another interior body would be useful.

• Purdue will hold its annual Fan Day and intrasquad exhibition on Oct. 21. Time has not been set. Football plays at Rutgers that day at noon, and current plans are to show the football game on the new video screen at Mackey Arena.

All of the new interior electronics are already operational.

• All four seniors — Vincent Edwards, Isaac Haas, Dakota Mathias and P.J. Thompson — will represent Purdue at Big Ten Media Day at Madison Square Garden in New York next month. It's a return trip for Edwards and Haas.

The Journal & Courier plans to attend as well.

Sept. 12: Time and TV set for Purdue vs. Louisville in Big Ten/ACC Challenge

Purdue's Nov. 28 home game vs. Louisville as part of the Big Ten/ACC Challenge will tip off at 8 p.m. and be broadcast by ESPN, the conference announced today.

The Boilermakers played at the KFC Yum! Center last season as part of the same event. Purdue had won seven straight in the challenge prior to that 71-64 loss.

The rest of the Big Ten/ACC Challenge schedule:

Notre Dame at Michigan State, 7 p.m., ESPN

• Tuesday update: Two more tipoff times announced—at Marquette on Nov. 14 (8:30 p.m.) and vs. Butler in the Crossroads Classic on Dec. 16 (noon).

Sept. 7: Boilermakers add Andrew McClatchey as staff analyst

Purdue basketball has hired Andrew McClatchey to its newly created analyst position, per the athletic department staff directory (and his Twitter bio).

McClatchey previously worked in an analyst capacity at both IUPUI and Anderson University. Per his IUPUI bio, he began working on basketball analytics during his high school days at New Palestine. He holds undergraduate and graduate degrees in mechanical engineering from IUPUI, with minors in mathematics and economics.

This is the first time Purdue has added a full-time analyst. Previously those duties were split up among the rest of the coaching staff.

The original job posting described the analysts's duties as: "develop algorithms and formulas to help define and translate statistical data into meaningful information to aid coaches in game strategy and team analysis."

PURDUE BASKETBALL: Boilermakers among Big Ten programs investing more in analytics

EDIT: On Friday, Purdue officially announced the additions of McClatchey and graduate assistants DJ Byrd and Joey Brooks (info below from Aug. 14 post).

"They have already started making a difference in our program and are eager to help us take the next step," Painter said in the news release. "They will be great additions to our family."

ICYMI: My sit-down with DJ Byrd over the summer:

BIG TEN ANNOUNCES TIMES, TELEVISION DESIGNATIONS

Per a Big Ten Conference release, here are most of Purdue's TV assignments for 2017-18. The most interesting nugget: the lone meeting with Indiana, in Bloomington on Jan. 28, is a 3:30 p.m. tipoff on FOX.
